topics.title: Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Recurrence Relation in Algorithm Analysis?

A **recurrence relation** is a mathematical equation or inequality that describes a function's value in terms of **its own previous (smaller) values**, combined with a base case. In the design and analysis of algorithms, recurrences are the formal language used to capture the **time complexity** of divide-and-conquer and recursive algorithms.

For a divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits a problem of size $n$ into $a$ sub-problems of size $n/b$ and spends $f(n)$ time in the divide/combine steps, the running time is modelled as:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

with a base case $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ (or $T(n) = \Theta(1)$ for small $n$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *recurrence* expresses $T(n)$ — the cost of an algorithm on input size $n$ — in terms of the cost $T(n/b)$ on a strictly smaller sub-problem, plus a non-recursive overhead $f(n)$. The four classical techniques to **solve** (i.e., find the closed-form asymptotic bound of) such recurrences are: **Iteration, Recursion Tree, Substitution, and Master's Theorem**.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Russian Doll of Computation**
> Imagine you are stacking nested boxes where each box takes time to **open** (the non-recursive cost $f(n)$) and produces **two smaller boxes** (the recursive calls). The total effort is the cost of *opening* every box at *every* level, summed across all levels, until you reach the smallest boxes (base case). The four methods are simply four different **ways of counting and summing** that total effort:
> - **Iteration** = manually open and re-write every nested box until you reach the smallest.
> - **Recursion Tree** = draw each layer of boxes on a whiteboard and add the costs *level by level*.
> - **Substitution** = make a smart guess of the final answer, then prove it by induction.
> - **Master's Theorem** = use a pre-derived lookup table (case 1 / 2 / 3) once you recognise the shape of the boxes.

## 1.3 Why These Four Methods? The High-Level Goal

All four methods target the same objective — to determine the **asymptotic growth class** $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$, $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$, or $\Omega(\cdot)$ — but differ in:

- **Precision**: Master's Theorem gives an instant bound; the others may yield tighter constants.
- **Generality**: Master's Theorem only works for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ form. The other three are general-purpose.
- **Ease of use**: Master's Theorem > Recursion Tree > Iteration > Substitution.

> [!TIP]
> KTU valuation rewards correctness, so **Master's Theorem should be your first attempt**. If the recurrence does not fit the canonical form (e.g., $aT(n/b) + f(n)$ but with $f(n)$ violating regularity, or non-uniform splits), fall back to Recursion Tree or Substitution.

## 1.4 The Canonical Recurrence Form

The standard form expected by the Master's Theorem is:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n) \quad \text{where } a \geq 1,\; b > 1$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | Number of sub-problems generated | $1, 2, 3, 4, \dots$ |
| $b$ | Factor by which sub-problem shrinks | $b > 1$ (often $2$) |
| $f(n)$ | Cost of divide + combine work outside recursion | $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ or similar |
| $T(1)$ | Base case cost (constant) | $\Theta(1)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions** ($T(0)$, $T(1)$) are essential for solving recurrences. The Master's Theorem is a **theoretical bound** that assumes $T(n) = \Theta(1)$ for sufficiently small $n$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — The Iteration Method (Unfolding / Repeated Substitution)

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The **Iteration Method** is a brute-force but reliable technique. You mechanically **unfold** the recurrence $i$ times, look for a pattern in the partial sums, and then sum the geometric (or non-geometric) series that emerges.

1. **Expand** the recurrence $i$ times so the right-hand side contains $T(n/b^i)$.
2. **Identify the pattern** in the additive term $f(n) + \frac{a}{b}f\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + \frac{a^2}{b^2}f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^2}\right) + \dots$
3. **Apply the base case** when $n/b^i = 1$ (or any constant), giving $i = \log_b n$.
4. **Evaluate the resulting sum** using geometric series or integral bounds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** The expansion produces a *telescoping* or *geometric* series whose closed form can be summed analytically.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Iteration Method

| Component | Expression |
|---|---|
| Initial recurrence | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ |
| After $i$ iterations | $T(n) = a^i T(n/b^i) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} a^j f(n/b^j)$ |
| Number of levels (depth) | $i^\star = \log_b n$ |
| Base contribution | $a^{\log_b n} T(1) = n^{\log_b a} \cdot T(1)$ |
| Total cost | $T(n) = n^{\log_b a} \cdot T(1) + \sum_{j=0}^{\log_b n - 1} a^j f(n/b^j)$ |

> **Real-world utility:** Iteration is invaluable when the recurrence has an **irregular** form (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$) where Master's Theorem fails. It is the only universal method.

## 2.2 Method 2 — The Recursion Tree Method

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The **Recursion Tree** is a visual + numerical hybrid: you draw a tree where each node represents a sub-problem's cost, and then sum costs **level by level**.

1. **Root node** — represents $f(n)$ at level $0$ (or the total cost $n$ depending on convention).
2. **Children** — for each recursive call, draw a child node representing $f(n/b)$, $f(n/b^2)$, etc.
3. **Per-level cost** — multiply the number of nodes at level $i$ by the per-node cost at that level.
4. **Leaf level** — the bottom of the tree; cost is the base case $\Theta(1)$ multiplied by the number of leaves $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$.
5. **Sum all levels** to obtain the total cost $T(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** It converts the recursive cost into a finite sum over tree levels, which can then be bounded using standard summations or integrals.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Recursion Tree

| Tree Property | Formula |
|---|---|
| Number of nodes at level $i$ | $a^i$ |
| Cost per node at level $i$ | $f(n/b^i)$ |
| Total cost at level $i$ | $a^i \cdot f(n/b^i)$ |
| Number of leaves | $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ |
| Leaf-level cost | $n^{\log_b a} \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| Total $T(n)$ | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n - 1} a^i f(n/b^i) \; + \; \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |

> **Real-world utility:** Helps in **verifying** Master's Theorem results and is excellent for recurrences where $f(n)$ is not a pure polynomial (e.g., $f(n) = n \log n$).

## 2.3 Method 3 — The Substitution Method (Guess + Induction)

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The **Substitution Method** has two phases:

1. **Guess a bound** (e.g., $T(n) \leq c n \log n$ for some $c > 0$). The guess can come from a recursion tree sketch or prior experience.
2. **Prove by mathematical induction** that the guess is valid for all $n \geq n_0$.

The inductive proof has three parts:
- **Base case:** Verify the bound holds for $n = n_0$ (typically $n_0 = 1$ or $2$).
- **Inductive hypothesis:** Assume $T(k) \leq c \cdot g(k)$ for all $k < n$.
- **Inductive step:** Substitute the hypothesis into the recurrence and show $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for some $c$ and $n \geq n_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** The bound is provably valid for *all* $n$ — this is the **only** method that gives a formal proof rather than an empirical sketch. KTU values it highly for "Prove that..." type questions.

> **Real-world utility:** The substitution method is the **gold standard** for proving tight bounds when the recurrence is non-standard or Master's Theorem does not apply.

## 2.4 Method 4 — The Master's Theorem (Cookbook Solution)

### Master Theorem Statement (As per CLRS / KTU 2024)

Let $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$ be constants, let $f(n)$ be a function, and let $T(n)$ be defined on non-negative integers by the recurrence:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where we interpret $n/b$ to mean either $\lfloor n/b \rfloor$ or $\lceil n/b \rceil$. Then $T(n)$ has the following asymptotic bounds:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Let $c_{\text{crit}} = \log_b a$. Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{c_{\text{crit}}}$.

### The Three Cases (with regularity condition)

| Case | Condition on $f(n)$ | Result $T(n) =$ | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = \mathcal{O}\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}} - \varepsilon}\right)$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ | $\Theta\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}}}\right)$ | Recursion dominates; leaves win |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}}} \log^k n\right)$ for $k \geq 0$ | $\Theta\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}}} \log^{k+1} n\right)$ | Levels are balanced |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}} + \varepsilon}\right)$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ **AND** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ (regularity) | $\Theta(f(n))$ | Root dominates |

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Theorem

| Symbol | Definition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| $n^{\log_b a}$ | The **critical exponent** | The dividing line between cases |
| $\varepsilon$ | Any positive constant | Strict gap between $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$ |
| Regularity | $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for $c < 1$ and large $n$ | Required for Case 3 |
| $k$ | The exponent of the log factor in Case 2 | Increases by 1 in the result |

> **Real-world utility:** Used to instantly classify the time complexity of **merge sort** ($n \log n$), **binary search** ($\log n$), **Strassen's matrix multiplication** ($n^{2.807}$), and **Karatsuba multiplication** ($n^{1.585}$).

## 2.5 Comparison of All Four Methods

| Criterion | Iteration | Recursion Tree | Substitution | Master Theorem |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Difficulty | Medium | Easy | Hard | Easiest |
| Generality | General | General | General | Restricted (canonical form only) |
| Output | Exact / asymptotic bound | Asymptotic bound | Rigorous proof | Instant asymptotic bound |
| Requires guess? | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| KTU exam frequency | High | High | Medium | **Highest** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Strategy:** Always (1) try Master's Theorem first, (2) if it fails, draw the Recursion Tree, (3) verify the closed form by Iteration, and (4) if a rigorous proof is asked, finish with Substitution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory exhaustive content**: every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Iteration Method — Full Derivation for $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^2$

Here $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = cn^2$. We solve it step-by-step.

### Iteration 1 (unfold once)

$$T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^2$$

### Iteration 2 (unfold the recursive term)

$$T(n) = 3\left[3T(n/16) + c(n/4)^2\right] + cn^2$$
$$= 9T(n/16) + 3c(n/4)^2 + cn^2$$
$$= 9T(n/16) + 3c \cdot \frac{n^2}{16} + cn^2$$
$$= 9T(n/16) + \frac{3c}{16} n^2 + cn^2$$

### Iteration 3 (unfold again)

$$T(n) = 9\left[3T(n/64) + c(n/16)^2\right] + \frac{3c}{16} n^2 + cn^2$$
$$= 27T(n/64) + 9c(n/16)^2 + \frac{3c}{16} n^2 + cn^2$$
$$= 27T(n/64) + \frac{9c}{256} n^2 + \frac{3c}{16} n^2 + cn^2$$

### General Pattern (after $i$ iterations)

By inspection, after $i$ substitutions:

$$T(n) = 3^i T(n/4^i) + cn^2 \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^j$$

**Explanation of the pattern:** The $cn^2$ term from the original recurrence picks up a multiplier of $3$ at each level (from $a = 3$) and its argument is divided by $4$ (from $b = 4$), so the squared argument is $n^2/4^{2j} = n^2/16^j$, giving a coefficient of $3^j / 16^j = (3/16)^j$.

### Apply Base Case

The recursion stops when $n/4^i = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_4 n$. The base contribution is:

$$3^{\log_4 n} T(1) = n^{\log_4 3} \cdot T(1) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_4 3}\right)$$

### Evaluate the Geometric Sum

Since $3/16 < 1$, the geometric series converges:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^j = \frac{1 - (3/16)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - 3/16} < \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13}$$

This is a **constant** independent of $n$! So the sum evaluates to $\Theta(1)$.

### Final Closed Form

$$T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_4 3}\right) + cn^2 \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with Master's Theorem:** $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$. Since $f(n) = cn^2 = \Omega(n^{0.792 + \varepsilon})$ and $3 f(n/4) = 3 c (n/4)^2 = (3/16) cn^2 \leq c f(n)$ for $c \geq 3/16$ → **Case 3** applies → $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. ✅

---

## 3.2 Recursion Tree — Full Construction for $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$

This is an **unbalanced** divide (cannot use Master's Theorem). We build the tree level-by-level.

### Level 0 (Root)

Cost = $cn$. Total at this level = $cn$.

### Level 1

Two sub-problems: $T(n/3)$ and $T(2n/3)$.
Cost per sub-problem's "overhead" = $c(n/3)$ and $c(2n/3)$ respectively.
Total at this level = $c(n/3) + c(2n/3) = cn$.

### Level 2

Four sub-problems with sizes $n/9, 2n/9, 2n/9, 4n/9$.
Total at this level = $c(n/9 + 2n/9 + 2n/9 + 4n/9) = c(9n/9) = cn$.

### General Pattern

**Key observation:** At every level, the sum of the sizes of the sub-problems is $n$. Therefore the **per-level cost is exactly $cn$ at every level**.

### Number of Levels

The longest path from root to a leaf is the path that always takes the larger sub-problem (size $2n/3$). After $i$ levels, the sub-problem size is $(2/3)^i n$. Leaf is reached when $(2/3)^i n = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_{3/2} n$.

The shortest path takes the smaller sub-problem, reaching depth $\log_3 n$.

### Total Cost

$$T(n) = (\text{number of levels}) \times (\text{cost per level}) = cn \cdot \log_{3/2} n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

---

## 3.3 Substitution Method — Full Proof for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, prove $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$

### Step 1: Guess the Bound

From recursion tree intuition, $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$. We guess:

$$T(n) \leq c n \log_2 n \quad \text{for some } c > 0$$

### Step 2: Base Case

For $n = 1$: $T(1) = 1$. Need $1 \leq c \cdot 1 \cdot \log_2 1 = 0$. This is false because $\log_2 1 = 0$.

**Fix:** Choose $n_0 = 2$. For $n = 2$, we need $T(2) \leq c \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 2c$. With $T(2) = 2$ (assume), pick $c \geq 1$. ✅

### Step 3: Inductive Hypothesis

Assume for all $k < n$ that $T(k) \leq c k \log_2 k$.

### Step 4: Inductive Step

Substitute into the recurrence:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$$

Apply the inductive hypothesis to $T(n/2)$:

$$T(n/2) \leq c \cdot (n/2) \cdot \log_2(n/2) = c(n/2)(\log_2 n - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$T(n) \leq 2 \cdot c(n/2)(\log_2 n - 1) + n$$
$$= cn(\log_2 n - 1) + n$$
$$= cn \log_2 n - cn + n$$

We need this to be $\leq cn \log_2 n$, i.e.:

$$-cn + n \leq 0$$
$$n(1 - c) \leq 0$$
$$c \geq 1$$

So choosing $c = 1$ works for $n \geq 2$. The induction is complete.

$$\boxed{\therefore T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)}$$

---

## 3.4 Master Theorem — Full Application for $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + 1000n^3$

### Identify Parameters

$a = 8$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1000n^3$.

### Compute the Critical Exponent

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 8} = n^3$$

### Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$

$$f(n) = 1000 n^3 = \Theta(n^3) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$$

This is **exactly** Case 2 with $k = 0$.

### Result

$$T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n\right) = \Theta(n^3 \log n)$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU students frequently mis-apply Case 2 by missing the $k$ in $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$. Always check whether $f(n)$ has a $\log$ factor.

---

## 3.5 Master Theorem — Extended Worked Example: $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$

### Identify Parameters

$a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2 \log n$.

### Compute the Critical Exponent

$$n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$$

### Compare

We need to check whether $f(n) = n^2 \log n$ is polynomially larger than $n^2$. The $\log n$ factor is **not polynomial** — it grows slower than $n^\varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$.

**Formally:** $f(n) = n^2 \log n = \Theta(n^2 \log^1 n)$ — Case 2 with $k = 1$.

### Result

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^2 n)$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix (Which Method to Use?)

| Recurrence Shape | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $f(n)$ polynomial | **Master Theorem** | Instant classification |
| $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with irregular $f(n)$ (e.g., $n \log n$, $n / \log n$) | **Master Theorem** (Case 2 variants) or Recursion Tree | Need careful comparison |
| $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$ (unbalanced split) | **Recursion Tree** | Master Theorem does not apply |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ (decrease by constant) | **Iteration** | Not divide-and-conquer |
| "Prove that $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(f(n))$" | **Substitution** | Only method that gives formal proof |
| $T(n) = 2T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ (variable split) | **Substitution** (let $n = 2^m$) or Iteration | Non-standard form |

---

## 3.7 Summary Table — Quick Reference for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$

| Algorithm | $a$ | $b$ | $f(n)$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Case | $T(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary Search | $1$ | $2$ | $1$ | $n^0 = 1$ | 2 ($k=0$) | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Merge Sort | $2$ | $2$ | $n$ | $n^1$ | 2 ($k=0$) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Karatsuba | $3$ | $2$ | $n$ | $n^{1.585}$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{1.585})$ |
| Strassen | $7$ | $2$ | $n^2$ | $n^{2.807}$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{2.807})$ |
| Bubble Sort (rec. form) | $1$ | $1$ | $n^2$ | $n^0 = 1$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Quick Sort (best) | $2$ | $2$ | $n$ | $n$ | 2 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric** node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to comply with KTU rendering requirements.

## 4.1 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Level 0: Cost n, 1 node, Total n"]
    A1["Level 1: Cost n/2 each, 2 nodes, Total n"]
    A2["Level 2: Cost n/4 each, 4 nodes, Total n"]
    A3["Level 3: Cost n/8 each, 8 nodes, Total n"]
    AL["Level L: Cost 1 each, n nodes, Total n"]
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> AL
    SUM["Total T(n) = L * n = n * log2 n = Theta n log n"]
    AL --> SUM
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each level contributes exactly $n$ to the total cost. The depth of the tree is $\log_2 n$. Hence $T(n) = n \cdot \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

## 4.2 Method Selection Flowchart (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Input Recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + fn"]
    Q1{"Is recurrence in canonical form aT(n/b) + fn?"}
    Q2{"Is f(n) polynomial AND regular?"}
    C1["Case 1: f smaller than n^logb a, T = Theta n^logb a"]
    C2["Case 2: f matches n^logb a log^k n, T = Theta n^logb a log^k plus 1 n"]
    C3["Case 3: f dominates AND regular, T = Theta fn"]
    NOTAPPLY["Master Theorem FAILS, use Recursion Tree"]
    TREE["Build tree, sum per level, get Theta"]
    ITER["Use Iteration Method, unfold, sum series"]
    SUB["Make guess, prove by Induction Substitution"]
    DONE["Final Asymptotic Bound Tn"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2
    Q1 -- NO --> NOTAPPLY
    NOTAPPLY --> TREE
    TREE --> ITER
    ITER --> SUB
    SUB --> DONE
    Q2 -- CASE 1 --> C1 --> DONE
    Q2 -- CASE 2 --> C2 --> DONE
    Q2 -- CASE 3 --> C3 --> DONE
```

## 4.3 Unbalanced Recursion Tree for $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["ROOT: size n, cost cn"]
    L["LEFT: size n/3, cost cn/3"]
    LL["size n/9, cost cn/9"]
    LR["size 2n/9, cost 2cn/9"]
    RT["RIGHT: size 2n/3, cost 2cn/3"]
    RTL["size 2n/9, cost 2cn/9"]
    RTR["size 4n/9, cost 4cn/9"]
    L --> LL
    L --> LR
    R --> L
    R --> RT
    RT --> RTL
    RT --> RTR
    NOTE["Each level total = cn. Tree depth = log 3/2 n. Total = cn log 3/2 n = Theta n log n"]
    NOTE
```

## 4.4 Master Theorem Decision Schematic (The Three Cases)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP["f(n) versus n^logb a"]
    DIFF{"Polynomial gap?"}
    C1B["f much smaller: CASE 1, T = Theta n^logb a"]
    C2B["f matches times log^k: CASE 2, T = Theta n^logb a log^k plus 1 n"]
    C3B["f much larger AND regular: CASE 3, T = Theta fn"]
    INP --> DIFF
    DIFF -- f is polylog smaller --> C1B
    DIFF -- f matches --> C2B
    DIFF -- f is polylog larger --> C3B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Master's Theorem for solving recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$. List all three cases with the regularity condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Theorem Statement:** Let $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$ be constants and $f(n)$ a function. Define $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n$, with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$. Then:
> - **Case 1:** If $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
> - **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
> - **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ **and** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ and all sufficiently large $n$ (regularity condition), then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Substitution Method and the Master Theorem. When does the Master Theorem fail to apply?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Substitution Method | Master Theorem |
|---|---|---|
| Requires initial guess? | **Yes** | No (automatic) |
| Provides proof? | **Yes (by induction)** | No (only bound) |
| Applies to canonical form only? | No (general) | **Yes** |

**Master Theorem fails to apply when:**
1. $a < 1$ (not a divide-and-conquer with branching).
2. The recurrence is **not in the canonical form** $aT(n/b) + f(n)$ (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ — non-uniform split).
3. The function $f(n)$ is **not polynomially comparable** to $n^{\log_b a}$ (e.g., $f(n) = n^{\log_b a} / \log n$ — falls in the "gap" between cases).
4. $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ but $a$ or $b$ is not a constant (e.g., $a = n$, $b = 2$).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + 18n^2$ using the Master's Theorem. Identify which case applies and explain the reasoning.**

#### **Part (a) — Apply Master's Theorem (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
$a = 7$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 18n^2$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$$

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_2 7}$.**
$$f(n) = 18n^2 = \mathcal{O}\!\left(n^{2.807 - 0.807}\right) = \mathcal{O}\!\left(n^{\log_2 7 - \varepsilon}\right)$$
where $\varepsilon = 0.807 > 0$.

**Step 4: Apply Case 1.**
$$T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_2 7}\right) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$$

> **Model Answer (Part a):** The recurrence falls under **Case 1** of the Master's Theorem because the non-recursive cost $f(n) = 18n^2$ is polynomially smaller than $n^{\log_2 7}$. Hence the cost is dominated by the **leaf-level work** (the recursive sub-problems), and $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$.

#### **Part (b) — Verify with the Recursion Tree Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Draw the tree.**

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["L0: 18n^2, 1 node, total 18n^2"]
    L1["L1: 18(n/2)^2 = 4.5n^2, 7 nodes, total 31.5n^2"]
    L2["L2: 18(n/4)^2 = 1.125n^2, 49 nodes, total 55.125n^2"]
    L3["L3: 18(n/8)^2, 343 nodes, total ... shrinking"]
    LF["Lf: 18*1, n^2.807 leaves, total Theta n^2.807"]
    R --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> LF
```

**Step 2: Compute the geometric ratio between levels.**

Total cost at level $i$ = $7^i \cdot 18(n/2^i)^2 = 7^i \cdot 18 \cdot n^2 / 4^i = 18 n^2 \cdot (7/4)^i$.

**Step 3: Identify the dominant level.**

The ratio is $7/4 = 1.75 > 1$, so the **leaf level dominates**.

**Step 4: Sum the geometric series.**

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} 18 n^2 (7/4)^i + \text{leaf cost}$$

$$= 18n^2 \cdot \frac{(7/4)^{\log_2 n} - 1}{7/4 - 1} + \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$$

The first sum is dominated by its largest term, which is the leaf-level term $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$.

**Step 5: Conclude.**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})}$$

Both methods agree. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using:**
- **(a)** The **Iteration Method** (7 marks)
- **(b)** The **Recursion Tree Method** (7 marks)

#### **Part (a) — Iteration Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1: First iteration.**
$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$$

**Step 2: Second iteration.**
$$T(n) = 2[2T(n/4) + (n/2)\log(n/2)] + n \log n$$
$$= 4T(n/4) + n \log(n/2) + n \log n$$

**Step 3: Third iteration.**
$$T(n) = 4[2T(n/8) + (n/4)\log(n/4)] + n \log(n/2) + n \log n$$
$$= 8T(n/8) + n \log(n/4) + n \log(n/2) + n \log n$$

**Step 4: General pattern (after $i$ iterations).**
$$T(n) = 2^i T(n/2^i) + n \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \log(n/2^j)$$
$$= 2^i T(n/2^i) + n \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} [\log n - j]$$

**Step 5: Apply base case.** $i = \log_2 n$, $2^i T(n/2^i) = n \cdot T(1) = \Theta(n)$.

**Step 6: Evaluate the sum.**
$$\sum_{j=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} (\log_2 n - j) = \sum_{k=1}^{\log_2 n} k = \frac{(\log_2 n)(\log_2 n + 1)}{2} = \Theta((\log n)^2)$$

**Step 7: Final bound.**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n) + n \cdot \Theta((\log n)^2) = \boxed{\Theta(n (\log n)^2)}$$

#### **Part (b) — Recursion Tree Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Level-0 cost = $n \log n$.**

**Step 2: Level-1 cost = $2 \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2) = n(\log n - 1)$.**

**Step 3: Level-2 cost = $4 \cdot (n/4) \log(n/4) = n(\log n - 2)$.**

**Step 4: General level-$i$ cost = $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) \log(n/2^i) = n(\log n - i)$.**

**Step 5: Number of levels = $\log_2 n$.**

**Step 6: Sum all levels.**
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n(\log n - i) + \text{leaf cost}$$
$$= n \sum_{k=1}^{\log_2 n} k + \Theta(n)$$
$$= n \cdot \frac{\log_2 n (\log_2 n + 1)}{2} + \Theta(n)$$
$$= \boxed{\Theta(n (\log n)^2)}$$

Both methods yield $\Theta(n (\log n)^2)$. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Pitfall 1: Confusing $k$ in Case 2.** When $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$, students often write $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ instead of $\Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$. You **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Pitfall 2: Forgetting the regularity condition in Case 3.** Writing $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ without checking $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ is **incomplete**. Deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Pitfall 3: Applying Master's Theorem to non-canonical recurrences.** Recurrences like $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ are **not** in the $aT(n/b) + f(n)$ form. The Master Theorem **cannot** be applied — you must use the Recursion Tree. Deduct 2 marks for incorrect application.
> 4. **Pitfall 4: Substitution method without a base case.** Always verify the base case for $n = n_0$ before claiming the induction is complete. Deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Pitfall 5: Forgetting the constant factor in Iteration.** When summing $cn^2 \sum (3/16)^j$, students often forget to evaluate the geometric series or write the answer as $cn^2 \log n$ instead of $cn^2$. The series evaluates to a **constant**, not $\log n$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Memorise):**

- [ ] A **recurrence** describes $T(n)$ in terms of $T(n/b)$ plus non-recursive cost $f(n)$.
- [ ] **Canonical form** for Master's Theorem: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$.
- [ ] **Critical exponent** is $n^{\log_b a}$. This is the dividing line for all three cases.
- [ ] **Case 1** ($f \ll n^{\log_b a}$): $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ — leaves dominate.
- [ ] **Case 2** ($f \approx n^{\log_b a} \log^k n$): $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$ — levels balanced.
- [ ] **Case 3** ($f \gg n^{\log_b a}$, regular): $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ — root dominates.
- [ ] **Regularity condition** $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ for $c < 1$ is **mandatory** in Case 3.
- [ ] **Iteration Method** = unfold $i$ times, identify the geometric pattern, sum the series.
- [ ] **Recursion Tree Method** = sum costs level by level; leaf-level cost is $n^{\log_b a}$.
- [ ] **Substitution Method** = guess + mathematical induction (3 parts: base, hypothesis, step).
- [ ] **Merge Sort** is the canonical Case 2 example → $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- [ ] **Binary Search** is the canonical Case 2 (with $k=0$) example → $\Theta(\log n)$.
- [ ] **Strassen's** matrix multiplication is the canonical Case 1 example → $\Theta(n^{2.807})$.
- [ ] Master's Theorem **fails** for: (i) $a < 1$, (ii) non-canonical form, (iii) $f(n)$ in the "gap" between cases.
- [ ] For $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ (unbalanced), always use the **Recursion Tree**.
- [ ] **Always** verify the geometric sum in Iteration (e.g., $cn^2 \sum (3/16)^j = \Theta(cn^2)$, not $\Theta(cn^2 \log n)$).
- [ ] In the Substitution Method, the base case is at $n = 2$ (or higher) if $\log_2 1 = 0$ creates issues.
- [ ] Karatsuba multiplication: $a=3$, $b=2$, $f(n)=n$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) = \Theta(n^{1.585})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **AVL Tree** (named after inventors **Adelson-Velsky and Landis**, 1962) is a self-balancing **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in which the **heights of the two child subtrees of any node differ by at most one**. If at any time they differ by more than one, rebalancing is performed to restore this property. AVL trees maintain a strict **height-balance condition**, guaranteeing $O(\log n)$ time for search, insertion, and deletion in the worst case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AVL trees are the canonical example of *height-balanced* BSTs. They form the foundation for understanding more advanced balanced structures (Red-Black Trees, Treaps, Splay Trees) covered later in the course.

### 1.1.1 Balance Factor (BF)

The **Balance Factor** of a node $N$ is defined as the difference between the heights of its left and right subtrees:

$$BF(N) = h(\text{left subtree}) - h(\text{right subtree})$$

For an AVL tree, the balance factor of **every node** must lie in the closed set $\{-1, 0, +1\}$.

$$BF(N) \in \{-1,\ 0,\ +1\}\quad \forall\ N \in \text{Tree}$$

Any node with $BF \notin \{-1, 0, +1\}$ is said to be **unbalanced**, triggering a rotation to restore the invariant.

### 1.1.2 Height of an AVL Tree

The maximum height $h$ of an AVL tree with $n$ nodes is bounded by the Fibonacci relation. The minimum number of nodes $N(h)$ required for an AVL tree of height $h$ satisfies the recurrence:

$$N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$$

with base cases $N(0) = 0$ and $N(1) = 1$. Solving this yields:

$$N(h) \geq \phi^{h} \quad \text{where}\ \phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618\ \text{(Golden Ratio)}$$

Therefore, the height is $h = O(\log_{\phi} n) = O(\log n)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "See-Saw" Principle**
> Imagine a see-saw (teeter-totter) where a parent node is the pivot. To keep the see-saw steady and avoid it tipping over, the "weight" (number of nodes / subtree height) on the left and right arms must be roughly equal. If you add a new node that makes one side too heavy, the entire structure from the pivot downward is "rebalanced" by **rotating** subtrees — like sliding a heavier child over to the lighter side. The see-saw is then balanced again, ensuring no path from root to leaf becomes disproportionately long.

## 1.3 Properties of AVL Trees

| Property | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **BST Property** | For every node, all keys in left subtree $<$ node key $<$ all keys in right subtree |
| **Balance Property** | $\vert BF(N) \vert \leq 1$ for every node $N$ |
| **Height Bound** | $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ (tight bound) |
| **Search Cost** | $O(\log n)$ worst case |
| **Insertion Cost** | $O(\log n)$ plus at most 2 rotations |
| **Deletion Cost** | $O(\log n)$ plus up to $O(\log n)$ rotations |
| **Storage Overhead** | Each node stores height (or BF), typically 4 bytes extra |

## 1.4 Why AVL Trees? — Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard BST has worst-case search time $O(n)$ (degenerate tree, e.g., inserting sorted data). AVL trees guarantee $O(\log n)$ by enforcing strict balance, making them ideal for **databases, in-memory dictionaries, and lookup-intensive applications** where predictable performance is critical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Foundation

An AVL tree node typically maintains three pointers and one integer field:

```
        ┌──────────────┐
        │  key | bf    │
        │ h_left| h_rt │
        ├──────┼───────┤
        │ left │ right │
        │ child│ child │
        └──────┴───────┘
```

- **left child**, **right child**, **parent** (optional, for easier bottom-up updates)
- **height** (or **balance factor**) of the subtree rooted at that node

## 2.2 Balance Factor Update After Modification

When an insertion or deletion occurs, only the **ancestors** of the modified node may have their balance altered. Let `updateHeight(node)` be the function called on the way back up the recursion.

$$h(\text{node}) = 1 + \max\big(h(\text{node.left}),\ h(\text{node.right})\big)$$

$$BF(\text{node}) = h(\text{node.left}) - h(\text{node.right})$$

## 2.3 The Four Imbalance Cases & Their Rotations

After insertion, the imbalance always occurs in the subtree of the **first unbalanced ancestor** (closest to the newly inserted node) and is one of exactly **four** canonical cases. For deletion, imbalance may propagate up the tree, requiring **multiple** rotations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention:** The imbalance is named by the **path** from the unbalanced node $A$ down to the newly inserted/deleted node $X$. The first letter denotes the direction taken from $A$ to its child $B$; the second letter denotes the direction from $B$ to its grandchild (or the new node).

| Case | BF of $A$ | Position of $X$ w.r.t. $A$ | Rotation Type | Direction |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **LL** (Left-Left) | $+2$ | $X$ in left subtree of left child of $A$ | Single Right Rotation | $\rightarrow$ |
| **RR** (Right-Right) | $-2$ | $X$ in right subtree of right child of $A$ | Single Left Rotation | $\leftarrow$ |
| **LR** (Left-Right) | $+2$ | $X$ in right subtree of left child of $A$ | Left then Right Rotation | $\rightarrow\leftarrow$ |
| **RL** (Right-Left) | $-2$ | $X$ in left subtree of right child of $A$ | Right then Left Rotation | $\leftarrow\rightarrow$ |

## 2.4 Rotation Mechanics (Pseudocode-Free Logical Steps)

### 2.4.1 Single Right Rotation (LL Case) — "Push Left Child Up"

1. Let $A$ be the unbalanced node, $B$ be $A.\text{left}$.
2. $B$ becomes the new root of this subtree.
3. $A$ becomes the right child of $B$.
4. The previous right child of $B$ (call it $T_2$) becomes the new left child of $A$.
5. Update heights of $A$ first, then $B$.

### 2.4.2 Single Left Rotation (RR Case) — "Push Right Child Up"

1. Let $A$ be unbalanced, $B$ be $A.\text{right}$.
2. $B$ becomes the new root.
3. $A$ becomes the left child of $B$.
4. The previous left child of $B$ (call it $T_2$) becomes the new right child of $A$.
5. Update heights of $A$ first, then $B$.

### 2.4.3 Left-Right Rotation (LR Case)

This is a **double rotation**:
1. First perform a **Left Rotation** on $A.\text{left}$ (i.e., the left child of $A$).
2. Then perform a **Right Rotation** on $A$ itself.

### 2.4.4 Right-Left Rotation (RL Case)

This is also a **double rotation**:
1. First perform a **Right Rotation** on $A.\text{right}$.
2. Then perform a **Left Rotation** on $A$ itself.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Bound | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Balance Factor | $BF = h_L - h_R$ | Must be in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ |
| Tree Height | $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328$ | Tight worst-case upper bound |
| Min nodes for height $h$ | $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$ | Fibonacci-like recurrence; $N(0)=0, N(1)=1$ |
| Min nodes asymptotic | $N(h) \geq \phi^{h} - 1$ | $\phi = 1.618...$ Golden Ratio |
| Search Complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Worst-case guaranteed |
| Insertion Complexity | $O(\log n)$ | At most **2** rotations |
| Deletion Complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Up to $O(\log n)$ rotations |
| Space per node | $O(1)$ extra | Stores height or BF |
| Rotation types | 4 total | LL, RR, LR, RL |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Applications in Industry:**
> - **Database indexing** (e.g., PostgreSQL historically used AVL-style balancing for memory indexes)
> - **In-memory key-value stores** (Redis sorted sets use skip lists, conceptually similar)
> - **Compiler symbol tables** requiring fast insert and lookup
> - **Network routing tables** for longest-prefix matching
> - **File system directories** (NTFS, ReiserFS used balanced tree variants)
> - **Real-time systems** where *worst-case* $O(\log n)$ is mandatory (vs. Red-Black trees which give amortized bounds)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Height Recurrence Derivation

Let $N(h)$ be the **minimum** number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$. Consider a tree of height $h$ with the fewest possible nodes: its root has two subtrees, one of height $h-1$ and the other of height $h-2$ (they must differ by exactly 1, else we could reduce the total height). Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \\
N(0) &= 0 \quad \text{(empty tree, height 0 by convention)} \\
N(1) &= 1 \quad \text{(single node, height 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the first few values gives:

| $h$ | $N(h)$ | Computation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | base case |
| 1 | 1 | base case |
| 2 | 2 | $1 + 1 + 0$ |
| 3 | 4 | $1 + 2 + 1$ |
| 4 | 7 | $1 + 4 + 2$ |
| 5 | 12 | $1 + 7 + 4$ |
| 6 | 20 | $1 + 12 + 7$ |
| 7 | 33 | $1 + 20 + 12$ |

The ratio $N(h+1)/N(h)$ converges to $\phi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(h) &\geq \phi^{h} \\
\Rightarrow\ h &\leq \log_{\phi}(N(h)) \\
\Rightarrow\ h &= O(\log n) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — LL Rotation After Insertion

**Insert sequence:** $30, 20, 10$ into an initially empty AVL tree.

### Step 1: Insert 30
Tree has only one node. Balance Factor of 30 = 0. **Balanced.**

### Step 2: Insert 20
20 < 30, becomes left child. Heights: $h(20)=1$, $h(\text{nil})=0$. $BF(30) = 1 - 0 = 1$. **Balanced.**

### Step 3: Insert 10
10 < 30, 10 < 20, becomes left child of 20. Heights: $h(10)=1$, $h(\text{nil})=0$. 
- $BF(20) = 1 - 0 = 1$ (balanced)
- $BF(30) = 2 - 0 = 2$ (**UNBALANCED!**)

The imbalance is on the **left** side of 30, and the new node 10 is on the **left** side of 20 → **LL case**.

### Apply Right Rotation on node 30:

Before rotation:
```
        30  (BF=+2)
       /
      20  (BF=+1)
     /
    10
```

After right rotation on 30:
```
      20  (BF=0)
     /  \
    10   30
```

Heights updated: $h(10)=1,\ h(30)=1,\ h(20)=2$. All $BF \in \{-1,0,1\}$. **AVL property restored.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — RR Rotation After Insertion

**Insert sequence:** $10, 20, 30$.

### Steps:
1. Insert 10 → root, $BF=0$.
2. Insert 20 → right child of 10. $BF(10) = 0 - 1 = -1$. Balanced.
3. Insert 30 → right child of 20. 
   - $BF(20) = 0 - 1 = -1$. Balanced.
   - $BF(10) = 0 - 2 = -2$. **UNBALANCED!** New node 30 is in right-right path → **RR case**.

### Apply Left Rotation on node 10:

Before:
```
    10  (BF=-2)
      \
       20 (BF=-1)
         \
          30
```

After left rotation on 10:
```
       20 (BF=0)
      /  \
     10   30
```

**AVL property restored.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — LR Rotation After Insertion

**Insert sequence:** $30, 10, 20$.

### Steps:
1. Insert 30 → root.
2. Insert 10 → left child of 30. $BF(30)=1$. Balanced.
3. Insert 20 → 20 > 10, so right child of 10. 
   - $BF(10) = 0 - 1 = -1$. Balanced.
   - $BF(30) = 2 - 0 = 2$. **UNBALANCED!** Path from 30 to 20: Left (to 10), then Right (to 20) → **LR case**.

### Apply LR Rotation (Left rotation on 10, then Right rotation on 30):

Before:
```
       30 (BF=+2)
      /
    10 (BF=-1)
      \
       20
```

After left rotation on 10:
```
       30 (BF=+2)
      /
     20 (BF=0)
     /
    10
```

After right rotation on 30:
```
      20 (BF=0)
     /  \
    10   30
```

**AVL property restored.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — RL Rotation After Insertion

**Insert sequence:** $10, 30, 20$.

### Steps:
1. Insert 10 → root.
2. Insert 30 → right child of 10. $BF(10) = -1$. Balanced.
3. Insert 20 → 20 < 30, left child of 30. 
   - $BF(30) = 1 - 0 = 1$. Balanced.
   - $BF(10) = 0 - 2 = -2$. **UNBALANCED!** Path from 10 to 20: Right (to 30), then Left (to 20) → **RL case**.

### Apply RL Rotation (Right rotation on 30, then Left rotation on 10):

Before:
```
    10 (BF=-2)
      \
      30 (BF=+1)
     /
    20
```

After right rotation on 30:
```
    10 (BF=-2)
      \
      20 (BF=0)
        \
         30
```

After left rotation on 10:
```
       20 (BF=0)
      /  \
     10   30
```

**AVL property restored.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Deletion With Rebalancing

**Initial AVL Tree:**
```
          50
        /    \
      30      70
     /  \    /  \
    20  40  60  80
```

Heights: $h(20)=h(40)=h(60)=h(80)=1$, $h(30)=h(70)=2$, $h(50)=3$. All BFs = 0.

### Task: Delete node 20

1. Find 20 (leaf, left child of 30). Remove it. Now $h(30.\text{left})=0$, $h(30.\text{right})=1$.
2. Update $BF(30) = 0 - 1 = -1$. Still balanced.
3. Update $h(30) = 2$, $h(50) = 3$. $BF(50) = 2 - 2 = 0$. Tree remains balanced.

Resulting tree:
```
          50
        /    \
      30      70
        \    /  \
        40  60  80
```

### Task: Delete node 30 (now)

1. Find 30. It has only a right child (40). Replace 30 with 40.
2. Now: left subtree of 50 has root 40 (height 1), right subtree has root 70 (height 2). $BF(50) = 1 - 2 = -1$. Balanced.
3. $h(50) = 3$. Tree remains valid.

Resulting tree:
```
          50
        /    \
      40      70
            /  \
           60  80
```

### Task: Delete node 60 (triggers rebalancing)

1. Find 60 (leaf, left child of 70). Remove it. 
2. $h(70) = 2$ (now from right child 80 only). $h(50.\text{left})=1$, $h(50.\text{right})=2$. $BF(50) = 1 - 2 = -1$. Balanced.
3. $h(50)=3$. Tree remains valid.

Resulting tree:
```
          50
        /    \
      40      70
              \
              80
```

### Task: Delete node 40 — Now triggers RR rotation

1. Remove 40. Now 50 has no left child. $h(50.\text{left})=0$, $h(50.\text{right})=2$.
2. $BF(50) = 0 - 2 = -2$. **UNBALANCED!**
3. Check child 70. Its $BF(70) = 0 - 1 = -1$ (right-heavy). Same sign as parent's imbalance → **RR case**.

### Apply Left Rotation on node 50:

Before:
```
    50 (BF=-2)
       \
       70 (BF=-1)
         \
         80
```

After left rotation on 50:
```
       70 (BF=0)
      /  \
     50   80
```

**AVL property restored.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Deletion Algorithm — Key Recursion Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Difference from Insertion:**
> - **Insertion:** Only **one rotation** is needed (at the *first* unbalanced ancestor going up). The tree's height does not increase after the rotation, so no further ancestors need rebalancing.
> - **Deletion:** A rotation may **reduce** the height of the replaced subtree, causing the imbalance to **propagate upward** all the way to the root. Hence, deletion may require up to $O(\log n)$ rotations.

### Generic Deletion Steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:}\ \text{Locate node } N \text{ to delete.} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:}\ \text{If } N \text{ has two children, replace with inorder successor (or predecessor).} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:}\ \text{Physically delete } N \text{ (now has } \leq 1 \text{ child).} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:}\ \text{Walk back from the parent of the deleted node, updating heights.} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:}\ \text{If a node } A \text{ has } BF(A) = \pm 2 \text{, determine case (LL/RR/LR/RL) by inspecting } A\text{'s taller child and grandchild.} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:}\ \text{Apply appropriate rotation(s).} \\
&\textbf{Step 7:}\ \text{Continue walking up to root, repeating Steps 5–6 if needed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Complexity Summary Table

| Operation | Time Complexity | Number of Rotations | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Search | $O(\log n)$ | 0 | BST search |
| Insert | $O(\log n)$ | $\leq 2$ | At most one rebalance point |
| Delete | $O(\log n)$ | $\leq O(\log n)$ | May cascade up to root |
| Rotation (LL/RR) | $O(1)$ | — | Pointer swaps only |
| Rotation (LR/RL) | $O(1)$ | — | Two single rotations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four Rotation Cases — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following diagram maps the **decision logic** for selecting and applying the correct rotation based on the balance factor pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["New Node Inserted or Deleted"] --> B{"Walk up to Root\nand update heights"}
    B --> C{"Is Balance Factor\n= +2 or -2?"}
    C -- "No, BF in -1, 0, 1" --> D["Node is Balanced\nContinue Upward"]
    C -- "Yes, BF = +2" --> E{"Left Child BF\nsign?"}
    C -- "Yes, BF = -2" --> F{"Right Child BF\nsign?"}
    E -- "BF >= 0 (LL or equal)" --> G["Single RIGHT Rotation\non unbalanced node"]
    E -- "BF < 0 (LR pattern)" --> H["Left Rotation on left child\nthen Right Rotation on node"]
    F -- "BF <= 0 (RR or equal)" --> I["Single LEFT Rotation\non unbalanced node"]
    F -- "BF > 0 (RL pattern)" --> J["Right Rotation on right child\nthen Left Rotation on node"]
    G --> K["Update Heights\nCheck Parent"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 AVL Tree State Transitions — All Four Rotations

This unified Mermaid diagram shows the **before** and **after** tree topology for each of the four canonical rotation cases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LL_CASE["LL Case - Single Right Rotation"]
        LL1["A node BF=+2"] --> LL2["B node left child of A"]
        LL2 --> LL3["X new node in B.left"]
        LL1Rot["B becomes root\nA becomes B.right\nT2 becomes A.left"] -.-> LLAfter["B at top\nA right child of B\nX remains in B.left"]
    end

    subgraph RR_CASE["RR Case - Single Left Rotation"]
        RR1["A node BF=-2"] --> RR2["B node right child of A"]
        RR2 --> RR3["X new node in B.right"]
        RR1Rot["B becomes root\nA becomes B.left\nT2 becomes A.right"] -.-> RRAfter["B at top\nA left child of B\nX remains in B.right"]
    end

    subgraph LR_CASE["LR Case - Double Rotation"]
        LR1["A node BF=+2"] --> LR2["B node left child of A"]
        LR2 --> LR3["C node right child of B"]
        LR3 --> LR4["X new node in C.left or right"]
        LR1Rot["Left rotate B\nthen Right rotate A\nC becomes root"] -.-> LRAfter["C at top\nB left child\nA right child"]
    end

    subgraph RL_CASE["RL Case - Double Rotation"]
        RL1["A node BF=-2"] --> RL2["B node right child of A"]
        RL2 --> RL3["C node left child of B"]
        RL3 --> RL4["X new node in C.left or right"]
        RL1Rot["Right rotate B\nthen Left rotate A\nC becomes root"] -.-> RLAfter["C at top\nB right child\nA left child"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Insertion Walk-Through — Sequence Tree States

This flowchart traces the states of the AVL tree as the sequence **30, 20, 10** is inserted (LL case scenario from Section 3.2).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Initial: Empty Tree"] --> S1["Insert 30\nState T1: Root is 30\nBF all nodes = 0"]
    S1 --> S2["Insert 20\nState T2: 20 is left child of 30\nBF 30 = +1, BALANCED"]
    S2 --> S3["Insert 10\nState T3 Temp: 10 is left child of 20\nBF 30 = +2, UNBALANCED!"]
    S3 --> S4{"Detect LL Case\non node 30"}
    S4 --> S5["Apply Right Rotation on 30\nState T4: 20 is new root\n10 is left of 20, 30 is right of 20\nAll BF = 0, BALANCED"]
```

## 4.4 Deletion Cascading Rebalance — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0["Tree Balanced Initially"] --> D1["Delete target node Z\nby BST rules"]
    D1 --> D2{"Did subtree height\ndecrease?"}
    D2 -- "No" --> D3["No rebalance needed\nTerminate"]
    D2 -- "Yes" --> D4["Move to parent P of\nmodified subtree"]
    D4 --> D5{"Is BF of P within\n-1, 0, +1?"}
    D5 -- "Yes" --> D6["Update height of P\nMove up to P.parent"]
    D5 -- "No, BF = +2" --> D7{"Check P.left BF\nto pick LL or LR"}
    D5 -- "No, BF = -2" --> D8{"Check P.right BF\nto pick RR or RL"}
    D7 -- "LL pattern" --> D9["Single Right Rotation on P"]
    D7 -- "LR pattern" --> D10["Left-Right Double Rotation on P"]
    D8 -- "RR pattern" --> D11["Single Left Rotation on P"]
    D8 -- "RL pattern" --> D12["Right-Left Double Rotation on P"]
    D9 --> D13{"Did rotation reduce\nsubtree height?"}
    D10 --> D13
    D11 --> D13
    D12 --> D13
    D13 -- "Yes" --> D4
    D13 -- "No" --> D3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an AVL tree. What is the balance factor of a node? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **AVL tree** is a height-balanced Binary Search Tree in which the heights of the left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most **one**. It was invented by Adelson-Velsky and Landis in 1962.

The **balance factor (BF)** of a node $N$ is defined as:

$$BF(N) = h(\text{left subtree of } N) - h(\text{right subtree of } N)$$

For an AVL tree, this factor must satisfy $BF(N) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ for every node $N$. The balance factor is used to detect when the tree becomes unbalanced after an insertion or deletion, triggering the appropriate rotation to restore the AVL property. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. List all four types of rotations used in AVL tree rebalancing. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four rotation types used in AVL trees are:

1. **LL (Left-Left) Rotation** — Single right rotation when the imbalance and new node are both in the left subtree path. (1 mark)
2. **RR (Right-Right) Rotation** — Single left rotation when both the imbalance and new node are in the right subtree path. (1 mark)
3. **LR (Left-Right) Rotation** — A left rotation on the left child followed by a right rotation on the unbalanced node. (0.5 mark)
4. **RL (Right-Left) Rotation** — A right rotation on the right child followed by a left rotation on the unbalanced node. (0.5 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following keys in the given order: 50, 20, 80, 10, 30, 70, 90, 25. Show the tree and the type of rotation (if any) at each step.**

**Model Solution:**

We insert keys one by one, applying rotations when balance factor becomes $\pm 2$.

**Insert 50:** Tree: `50`. BF(50)=0. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 20:** 20<50, left child. Tree:
```
    50
   /
  20
```
BF(50)=+1, BF(20)=0. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 80:** 80>50, right child. Tree:
```
    50
   /  \
  20   80
```
All BFs=0. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 10:** 10<50, 10<20, left child of 20. BF(20)=+1, BF(50)=+1. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 30:** 30<50, 30>20, right child of 20. BF(20)=−1, BF(50)=+1. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 70:** 70>50, 70<80, left child of 80. BF(80)=+1, BF(50)=0. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 90:** 90>50, 90>80, right child of 80. 
- BF(80) = 0−1 = −1. Balanced.
- BF(50) = 1−2 = −1. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 25:** 25<50, 25>20, 25<30, left child of 30. 
- BF(30) = 1−0 = +1. Balanced.
- BF(20) = 1−1 = 0. Balanced.
- BF(50) = 2−2 = 0. Balanced. *(No rotation)*

**Final AVL Tree:**
```
            50
          /    \
        20      80
       /  \    /  \
      10  30  70  90
          /
         25
```

All balance factors are within $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. The tree satisfies the AVL property. (7 marks — 1 mark per insertion step, 1 mark for final tree and verification)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the LL and RR rotation operations in AVL trees with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**LL Rotation (Single Right Rotation):** (3.5 marks)

This occurs when a new node is inserted into the **left subtree of the left child** of an unbalanced node $A$.

*Before rotation:*
```
        A (BF = +2)
       /
      B
     /
    T1   (B's left subtree, contains new node)
```
(plus T2 = B's right subtree and T3 = A's right subtree)

*After right rotation on A:*
```
        B
       / \
      T1  A
         / \
        T2  T3
```

The left child $B$ becomes the new root of the subtree. $A$ becomes $B$'s right child. $B$'s original right subtree $T_2$ becomes $A$'s new left subtree. Heights are updated bottom-up.

**RR Rotation (Single Left Rotation):** (3.5 marks)

This occurs when a new node is inserted into the **right subtree of the right child** of an unbalanced node $A$.

*Before rotation:*
```
    A (BF = -2)
      \
       B
        \
         T3  (B's right subtree, contains new node)
```
(plus T1 = A's left subtree and T2 = B's left subtree)

*After left rotation on A:*
```
        B
       / \
      A   T3
     / \
    T1  T2
```

The right child $B$ becomes the new root. $A$ becomes $B$'s left child. $B$'s original left subtree $T_2$ becomes $A$'s new right subtree. Heights are updated bottom-up.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Insert the keys 10, 20, 30, 25, 27, 7, 8 into an initially empty AVL tree. Show all intermediate trees and identify each rotation performed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Insert 10:** Tree: `10`. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 20:** 20>10, right child. BF(10)=−1. *(No rotation)*

**Insert 30:** 30>10, 30>20, right child of 20. 
- BF(20) = −1. 
- BF(10) = 0−2 = **−2**. 
- Path 10→20→30 = Right-Right → **Apply Left Rotation on 10 (RR case).**

After RR rotation on 10:
```
      20
     /  \
    10   30
```

**Insert 25:** 25>20, 25<30, left child of 30. 
- BF(30) = +1. 
- BF(20) = 1−1 = 0. 
- *(No rotation needed)*

```
      20
     /  \
    10   30
        /
       25
```

**Insert 27:** 27>20, 27<30, 27>25, right child of 25. 
- BF(25) = −1. 
- BF(30) = 1−1 = 0. 
- BF(20) = 1−1 = 0. 
- *(No rotation)*

```
      20
     /  \
    10   30
        /
       25
         \
          27
```

**Insert 7:** 7<20, 7<10, left child of 10. BF(10)=+1, BF(20)=+1. *(No rotation)*

```
        20
       /  \
      10   30
     /     /
    7     25
           \
            27
```

**Insert 8:** 8<20, 8<10, 8>7, right child of 7. 
- BF(7) = −1. 
- BF(10) = 1−0 = +1. 
- BF(20) = 2−1 = **+2**. 
- Path from 20: Left (to 10), then Right (to 8) → **LR case. Apply Left-Right rotation on 20.**

*Step 1: Left rotation on 10 (left child of 20):*
```
        20
       /  \
      8    30
     / \   /
    7  10 25
           \
            27
```
*Step 2: Right rotation on 20:*
```
          8
         / \
        7   20
           /  \
          10   30
              /
             25
              \
               27
```

**Final AVL Tree:** All BFs in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. AVL property satisfied. (7 marks — 1 mark per insertion, including identification of rotation type and resulting tree)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Delete the keys 25 and 7 successively from the final AVL tree above. Show all rebalancing steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Starting Tree:**
```
          8
         / \
        7   20
           /  \
          10   30
              /
             25
              \
               27
```

**Delete 25:**

Step 1: 25 is a leaf (left child of 30). Simply remove it.
Step 2: Now 30 has only right child 27. Heights: $h(27)=1$, $h(30)=2$, $h(20)=3$, $h(8)=4$. 
- BF(30) = 0−1 = −1. Balanced.
- BF(20) = 1−2 = −1. Balanced.
- BF(8) = 1−3 = **−2**. UNBALANCED!
- Check 20's BF = −1 (right-heavy). Same sign as parent → **RR case.**

*Apply Left Rotation on 8:*
```
        20
       /  \
      8    30
     / \     \
    7  10    27
```

**Delete 7:**

Step 1: 7 is a leaf (left child of 8). Remove it.
Step 2: Heights: $h(8)=1$, $h(10)=1$, $h(30)=2$, $h(20)=3$.
- BF(8) = 0. Balanced.
- BF(20) = 2−2 = 0. Balanced.

**Final Tree:**
```
        20
       /  \
      8    30
       \     \
       10    27
```

All balance factors within $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. AVL property maintained. (7 marks — 3.5 marks per deletion, including identification of rotation case and final tree)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update heights** after a rotation — heights must be updated bottom-up: child first, then parent. Examiners explicitly check the height field. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong rotation identification** — students often confuse LR with LL. Always check the path from the unbalanced node to the newly inserted/deleted node step by step.
> 3. **Ignoring the parent pointer update** — after rotation, the new subtree root's parent must point to the grandparent. Drawing the tree without showing the parent connection loses partial credit.
> 4. **For deletion, missing cascading rotations** — only stating "rotation applied" without continuing to walk up the tree to check for further imbalances. **Deletion may need $O(\log n)$ rotations, not just one.**
> 5. **Not verifying the final tree's balance factors** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for an explicit verification statement: "All BFs are in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$, hence AVL property is satisfied."
> 6. **Off-by-one in height computation** — be consistent: an empty tree has height 0, a single node has height 1. Mixing conventions causes cascading errors.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AVL Trees:**

- **Definition:** Self-balancing BST where every node has $\vert BF \vert \leq 1$.
- **Balance Factor:** $BF = h_L - h_R \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ for all nodes.
- **Height Bound:** $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$, hence all operations are $O(\log n)$.
- **Four Rotations:** LL (single right), RR (single left), LR (left-then-right), RL (right-then-left).
- **Naming Trick:** The case name (LL/RR/LR/RL) describes the path from the unbalanced node to the new node.
- **Insertion Rebalancing:** Walk up from inserted node, apply rotation at **first unbalanced ancestor**. **At most 1 rotation (2 for LR/RL double rotation), height does not grow above original.**
- **Deletion Rebalancing:** Walk up from deleted node, **may need multiple rotations** cascading to root, up to $O(\log n)$ rotations.
- **Time Complexity:** Search = Insert = Delete = $O(\log n)$ worst case.
- **Space Overhead:** Stores height or BF (typically an `int` field, 4 bytes per node).
- **AVL vs Red-Black:** AVL is **more strictly balanced** (faster lookups), Red-Black is **loosely balanced** (faster insertions/deletions with fewer rotations). Choose AVL for **read-heavy** workloads.
- **Practical Tip:** Always **update heights** *after* a rotation in the order: child first, then parent. Verify with explicit BF calculation in the final answer.
- **Draw cleanly:** Use left/right child conventions consistently. KTU examiners reward clearly drawn trees with labels on each node.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BFS (Breadth-First Search)** is a **systematic graph traversal algorithm** that explores all vertices of a graph at the **present depth level** before moving on to the vertices at the **next depth level**. It was formulated for unweighted shortest-path problems on graphs and is the cornerstone of many graph algorithms.

Formally, given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and a distinguished source vertex $s \in V$, BFS computes the **minimum number of edges (hops)** on every shortest path from $s$ to every reachable vertex $v \in V$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Ripples on a Pond"

Imagine you drop a stone into the still surface of a pond. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles**. At time $t = 0$, only the impact point is disturbed. At $t = 1$, the immediate neighbours are reached. At $t = 2$, the neighbours' neighbours are reached, and so on. **BFS behaves exactly like this ripple expansion** — it discovers the graph in *waves* of increasing distance from the source. A queue (FIFO structure) acts as the "shock front" of the wave.

### Key Properties of BFS

- Uses a **Queue (FIFO)** data structure.
- Computes **shortest path** (in number of edges) in **unweighted graphs**.
- Produces a **BFS Tree** containing the shortest-path predecessor of every reachable vertex.
- Time complexity: $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$.

---

## 1.2 Depth-First Search (DFS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFS (Depth-First Search)** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as **far as possible** along each branch before **backtracking**. It uses a **Stack (LIFO)** — either an explicit stack or the system's recursion call-stack.

DFS can be applied to both **directed** and **undirected** graphs, and to both **connected** and **disconnected** graphs. The output is a sequence of discovery and finishing times, which form the basis of powerful algorithms like **topological sorting**, **SCC detection**, and **cycle detection**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Exploring a Maze"

Imagine you are trapped in a maze. At every junction, you **mark the path you came from** and turn **left** (or right) and keep walking. When you hit a dead end, you **backtrack** to the last marked junction and try another path. DFS is the algorithmic embodiment of this **"go deep, then back up"** strategy. The call stack serves as your breadcrumb trail.

### Key Properties of DFS

- Uses a **Stack (LIFO)** or **recursion**.
- Produces a **DFS Forest** (one tree per connected component).
- Generates two timestamps per vertex: `d[v]` (discovery) and `f[v]` (finishing).
- Time complexity: $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$.

---

## 1.3 Strongly Connected Components (SCC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Strongly Connected Component (SCC)** of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal set of vertices** $C \subseteq V$ such that for **every pair** of vertices $u, v \in C$, there exists a path from $u \to v$ **and** from $v \to u$.

Equivalently, an SCC is a **maximal strongly connected subgraph**. The collection of all SCCs of $G$ forms a partition of $V$ (every vertex belongs to exactly one SCC). The **SCC graph** (or component graph) $G^{SCC}$ is a DAG whose nodes are the SCCs and whose edges represent inter-component reachability.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Social Media Echo Chambers"

In a social network where "following" is one-way, an SCC represents a **tightly-knit community** where, by following different chains of people, *anyone* in the group can reach *anyone else*. Twitter followers, hyperlinks between web pages, and road networks with one-way streets all decompose into such communities. The SCC graph then reveals the **hierarchical flow of influence** between these communities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to know **Tarjan's Algorithm** *or* **Kosaraju's Algorithm** for SCC detection, both of which run in $O(V + E)$ time by leveraging DFS-based computations on $G$ and its transpose $G^T$.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | **Queue (FIFO)** | **Stack / Recursion (LIFO)** |
| Traversal Order | **Level-order** (breadth-wise) | **Depth-wise** (go deep, then back) |
| Best For | **Shortest path** (unweighted) | **Cycle detection, topological sort, SCC** |
| Memory Profile | High (stores frontier) | Low (just the recursion stack) |
| Time Complexity | $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$ | $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$ |
| Output | BFS Tree | DFS Tree + timestamps |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BFS — Operational Mechanics

BFS maintains three states for each vertex, indicated by **colour codes**:

- ⚪ **WHITE** — undiscovered (not yet visited).
- ⚫ **GREY** — discovered, sitting in the queue (frontier).
- ⬛ **BLACK** — fully explored (all neighbours processed).

### Step-by-Step Logic of BFS

1. **Initialise** all vertices to WHITE, set `distance[s] = 0`, `parent[s] = NIL`.
2. **Enqueue** source $s$ and colour it GREY.
3. While the queue is **not empty**:
   - **Dequeue** vertex $u$.
   - For each neighbour $v$ of $u$ (scanning the adjacency list):
     - If $v$ is WHITE, then:
       - Colour $v$ **GREY**.
       - Set `distance[v] = distance[u] + 1`.
       - Set `parent[v] = u`.
       - **Enqueue** $v$.
   - Colour $u$ **BLACK**.
4. **Output** the BFS tree encoded by the `parent[]` array.

### The Core 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why queue?** Because we need to process vertices in the order they were discovered, which guarantees the first time we reach a vertex, it is via a shortest path.
- **Why colour BLACK after exploring all neighbours?** So that the vertex never gets re-added to the queue.
- **Why store `parent[]`?** It allows us to reconstruct the shortest path by following parents backwards from any target $v$ to the source $s$.

### BFS Shortest Path Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (BFS Shortest Path):** On an unweighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$, BFS computes for every vertex $v$, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ — the *true* shortest-path distance (minimum number of edges).

### BFS Tree Properties

- The edges $(parent[v], v)$ for all $v \neq s$ form a tree rooted at $s$ — the **BFS tree**.
- For any edge $(u, v)$ in the graph, the difference $\vert d[u] - d[v] \vert \leq 1$.
- Vertices in the BFS tree are partitioned into **levels** by their distance from $s$.

### Real-World Utility of BFS

- **GPS / Maps**: Finding the minimum number of intersections between two locations (unweighted road graphs).
- **Web Crawlers**: Discover all links reachable from a starting page.
- **Social Networks**: Find the *degree of separation* between two users (BFS level = "friend distance").
- **Broadcasting in Networks**: Minimum number of hops to deliver a packet.
- **AI / Game Solving**: Uniform-cost exploration in shallow state spaces.

---

## 2.2 DFS — Operational Mechanics

DFS also uses the **WHITE / GREY / BLACK** colour scheme but with the addition of **two timestamps per vertex**:

- $d[v]$ — **discovery time** (when $v$ turns GREY).
- $f[v]$ — **finishing time** (when $v$ turns BLACK, after all descendants are processed).

A global counter `time` is incremented at each event. The timestamps satisfy the canonical **"1 to 2V" bracket** property: for any $n$ vertices, the union of $[d[v], f[v]]$ over all $v$ forms a set of $2n$ distinct values in $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 2V\}$.

### Step-by-Step Logic of DFS (Recursive Form)

1. For every vertex $u$ in $V$:
   - If $u$ is WHITE, call `DFS-Visit(u)`.
2. `DFS-Visit(u)`:
   - Set $time \leftarrow time + 1$, $d[u] \leftarrow time$, colour $u$ **GREY**.
   - For each neighbour $v$ of $u$:
     - If $v$ is WHITE:
       - Set $parent[v] \leftarrow u$.
       - Recursively call `DFS-Visit(v)`.
   - Set $time \leftarrow time + 1$, $f[u] \leftarrow time$, colour $u$ **BLACK**.

### Classification of Edges in DFS

For a directed graph, DFS classifies every edge $(u, v)$ as one of four types:

- 🟢 **Tree Edge** — $(u, v)$ where $v$ is first discovered via $u$. Part of the DFS forest.
- 🔴 **Back Edge** — $(u, v)$ where $v$ is an **ancestor** of $u$ in the DFS tree. **Proof of cycle!**
- 🟡 **Forward Edge** — $(u, v)$ where $v$ is a **descendant** of $u$ (but not a tree edge).
- 🔵 **Cross Edge** — $(u, v)$ where $u$ and $v$ are in **different trees** or **different branches** of the same tree (neither ancestor nor descendant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In an undirected graph, every edge is either a *Tree Edge* or a *Back Edge*** — there are no forward or cross edges (because each undirected edge is examined from both endpoints).

### DFS Parenthesis Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Parenthesis):** For any two vertices $u$ and $v$, exactly one of the following holds:
> 1. The intervals $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are **disjoint** ($u$ and $v$ are unrelated in the DFS tree).
> 2. $[d[u], f[u]]$ is **entirely inside** $[d[v], f[v]]$ ($u$ is a descendant of $v$).
> 3. $[d[v], f[v]]$ is **entirely inside** $[d[u], f[u]]$ ($v$ is a descendant of $u$).

### Real-World Utility of DFS

- **Cycle Detection** — any back edge indicates a cycle.
- **Topological Sorting** — process vertices in **decreasing order of $f[v]$**.
- **SCC Detection** — Kosaraju's algorithm and Tarjan's algorithm rely on DFS.
- **Maze Solving / Path Finding**.
- **Compiler Design** — strongly connected region analysis, dead-code elimination.
- **Garbage Collection** — mark-and-sweep algorithms.

---

## 2.3 Strongly Connected Components — Operational Concepts

### Why SCC Detection Matters

Many real-world directed networks (the web, social media, software call graphs) decompose into *natural communities* (SCCs) connected by *one-way influence paths*. Analysing the **SCC DAG** gives the **macro structure** of the network.

### Kosaraju's Algorithm — Three Phases

1. Run DFS on $G$, computing finishing times $f[v]$ for all $v \in V$.
2. Compute the **transpose graph** $G^T$ (reverse every edge).
3. Run DFS on $G^T$, processing vertices in **decreasing order of $f[v]$ from Phase 1**.
4. Each DFS tree in Phase 3 = **one SCC**.

### Tarjan's Algorithm — Single-Pass Variant

- Maintains a **stack** of currently active vertices.
- Assigns each vertex two numbers: `index[v]` (DFS discovery order) and `lowlink[v]` (smallest index reachable via the DFS subtree).
- A vertex is the **root of an SCC** when `index[v] == lowlink[v]`. At that moment, pop the stack down to $v$ to extract the SCC.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these for any BFS / DFS / SCC KTU problem — they appear in nearly every question paper.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Property | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BFS Time Complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | BFS |
| 2 | DFS Time Complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | DFS |
| 3 | SCC Time Complexity (Kosaraju) | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | SCC |
| 4 | SCC Time Complexity (Tarjan) | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | SCC |
| 5 | BFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ (queue + colour) | BFS |
| 6 | DFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ (recursion stack) | DFS |
| 7 | BFS Shortest Path | $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ | BFS |
| 8 | Edge Level Invariant (BFS) | $\vert d[u] - d[v] \vert \leq 1$ | BFS Tree |
| 9 | Discovery Time Range | $1 \leq d[v] \leq 2\vert V \vert - 1$ | DFS |
| 10 | Finishing Time Range | $2 \leq f[v] \leq 2\vert V \vert$ | DFS |
| 11 | Parenthesis Bracket | $[d[v], f[v]]$ intervals | DFS |
| 12 | Cycle Test (undirected) | A back edge exists $\iff$ cycle | DFS |
| 13 | Cycle Test (directed) | A back edge exists $\iff$ cycle | DFS |
| 14 | Topological Sort Output | Vertices in decreasing $f[v]$ | DFS |
| 15 | Number of SCCs (worst case) | 1 (fully connected) to $\vert V \vert$ (no edges) | SCC |
| 16 | Sum of $\vert V_i \vert$ over all SCCs | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert V_i \vert = \vert V \vert$ | SCC Partition |
| 17 | White-Path Theorem | $v$ is a descendant of $u$ in DFS tree $\iff$ at time $d[u]$, a white path exists from $u$ to $v$ | DFS |
| 18 | Adjacency Matrix BFS/DFS | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ — list-based gives $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Both |
| 19 | BFS Tree edges | $\vert V \vert - 1$ (for connected graph) | BFS |
| 20 | DFS Number of Trees in Forest | Equals the number of connected components | DFS |

> [!NOTE]
> All listed complexities assume an **adjacency-list representation**. With an **adjacency matrix**, scan-cost becomes $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ regardless of $\vert E \vert$.
