# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Topological Sort — Source Removal (Kahn's) Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Given DAG G = V,E]) --> initA[Compute in-degree for every vertex v in V]
    initA --> queueA[Initialize empty queue Q]
    queueA --> scanA{For every vertex v}
    scanA -->|indeg v = 0| enqA[Enqueue v into Q]
    scanA -->|indeg v greater than 0| skipA[Skip v]
    enqA --> nextA{All vertices scanned?}
    skipA --> nextA
    nextA -->|No| scanA
    nextA -->|Yes| loopA{Q empty?}
    loopA -->|Yes| checkA{Output size equals V?}
    checkA -->|Yes| successA([Success: Topological Order found])
    checkA -->|No| cycleA([Failure: Graph has a cycle])
    loopA -->|No| deqA[Dequeue vertex u from Q]
    deqA --> appendA[Append u to output list L]
    appendA --> iterA{For each outgoing edge u to v}
    iterA -->|Edge exists| decA[Decrement indeg v by 1]
    decA --> checkB{indeg v equals 0 now?}
    checkB -->|Yes| enqB[Enqueue v into Q]
    checkB -->|No| iterA
    enqB --> iterA
    iterA -->|All edges processed| loopA
```

## 4.2 Topological Sort — DFS Finish-Time Stack Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: For each unvisited vertex u]) --> markB[Mark u as visited]
    markB --> loopB{For every neighbor v of u}
    loopB -->|v unvisited| recurseB[Recurse DFS v]
    recurseB --> loopB
    loopB -->|All neighbors done| pushB[Push u onto stack]
    pushB --> moreB{More unvisited vertices?}
    moreB -->|Yes| startB
    moreB -->|No| popB[Pop all vertices from stack]
    popB --> orderB([Topological Order = Pop sequence])
```

## 4.3 Generic D&C Control Abstraction — Recursion Skeleton

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dcStart([Call DandC on problem P of size n]) --> baseCheck{Is n less than or equal to n0?}
    baseCheck -->|Yes| adhoc[Return AdHoc P]
    baseCheck -->|No| divide[Divide P into k sub-problems P1 to Pk each of size n over b]
    divide --> recurse1[Recurse: Y1 equals DandC P1]
    recurse1 --> recurse2[Recurse: Y2 equals DandC P2]
    recurse2 --> recurseDots[Recurse dots Recurse Yk equals DandC Pk]
    recurseDots --> combine[Combine Y1 Y2 through Yk into final result]
    combine --> dcReturn([Return combined result])
    adhoc --> dcReturn
```

## 4.4 Merge Sort — Recursion Tree Diagram (for $n = 8$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN([T of n: 1 node size 8 cost 8c])
    rootN --> L1A([T of n/2: 1 node size 4 cost 4c])
    rootN --> L1B([T of n/2: 1 node size 4 cost 4c])
    L1A --> L2A([T of n/4: 1 node size 2 cost 2c])
    L1A --> L2B([T of n/4: 1 node size 2 cost 2c])
    L1B --> L2C([T of n/4: 1 node size 2 cost 2c])
    L1B --> L2D([T of n/4: 1 node size 2 cost 2c])
    L2A --> L3A([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2A --> L3B([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2B --> L3C([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2B --> L3D([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2C --> L3E([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2C --> L3F([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2D --> L3G([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    L2D --> L3H([T of 1: 1 node cost c])
    levelNote[/Level 0: 8c, Level 1: 8c, Level 2: 8c, Level 3: 8c. Total = 8c times 4 = 32c = 8 times 4 times log2 8 = 32c/]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The total cost is $4 \times 8c = 32c$, matching $n \log_2 n \cdot c = 8 \cdot 3 \cdot c = 24c$ asymptotically. The leading constant is governed by $c \cdot n \cdot (\text{number of non-leaf levels} + 1)$.

## 4.5 Strassen's — Block Partitioning of $n \times n$ Matrices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph matA[Matrix A partitioned into 4 blocks]
        A11[Block A11: top-left n/2 by n/2]
        A12[Block A12: top-right n/2 by n/2]
        A21[Block A21: bottom-left n/2 by n/2]
        A22[Block A22: bottom-right n/2 by n/2]
    end
    subgraph matB[Matrix B partitioned into 4 blocks]
        B11[Block B11: top-left n/2 by n/2]
        B12[Block B12: top-right n/2 by n/2]
        B21[Block B21: bottom-left n/2 by n/2]
        B22[Block B22: bottom-right n/2 by n/2]
    end
    subgraph matC[Output Matrix C partitioned into 4 blocks]
        C11[Block C11]
        C12[Block C12]
        C21[Block C21]
        C22[Block C22]
    end
    subgraph mProds[Strassen 7 sub-products]
        M1[M1 = A11 + A22 times B11 + B22]
        M2[M2 = A21 + A22 times B11]
        M3[M3 = A11 times B12 - B22]
        M4[M4 = A22 times B21 - B11]
        M5[M5 = A11 + A12 times B22]
        M6[M6 = A21 - A11 times B11 + B12]
        M7[M7 = A12 - A22 times B21 + B22]
    end
    A11 --> M1
    A22 --> M1
    B11 --> M1
    B22 --> M1
    A21 --> M2
    A22 --> M2
    B11 --> M2
    A11 --> M3
    B12 --> M3
    B22 --> M3
    A22 --> M4
    B21 --> M4
    B11 --> M4
    A11 --> M5
    A12 --> M5
    B22 --> M5
    A21 --> M6
    A11 --> M6
    B11 --> M6
    B12 --> M6
    A12 --> M7
    A22 --> M7
    B21 --> M7
    B22 --> M7
    M1 --> C11
    M4 --> C11
    M5 --> C11
    M7 --> C11
    M3 --> C12
    M5 --> C12
    M2 --> C21
    M4 --> C21
    M1 --> C22
    M2 --> C22
    M3 --> C22
    M6 --> C22
```

## 4.6 Master Theorem — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mtStart([Given T of n = aT of n over b + f of n]) --> critExp[Compute n to the power log base b of a]
    critExp --> compare1{Is f of n polynomially smaller than n to the power log base b of a?}
    compare1 -->|Yes, Case 1| case1[T of n = Theta of n to the power log base b of a]
    compare1 -->|No| compare2{Is f of n polynomially equal to n to the power log base b of a times log to the k of n?}
    compare2 -->|Yes, Case 2| case2[T of n = Theta of n to the power log base b of a times log to the k+1 of n]
    compare2 -->|No| compare3{Is f of n polynomially larger than n to the power log base b of a AND regularity holds?}
    compare3 -->|Yes, Case 3| case3[T of n = Theta of f of n]
    compare3 -->|No| gap([Master Theorem does not apply: use recursion tree or Akra-Bazzi])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, Module 2]**

### Q1. Define Topological Sorting. State any **two** applications where it is used. `(3 marks)`

**Model Answer:**

> Topological sorting is the process of arranging the vertices of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** in a linear order such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$, vertex $u$ appears before $v$. It exists **if and only if** the graph has no directed cycle.
>
> **Applications (any two):**
> 1. **Build systems / Makefile compilation** — source files must be compiled in dependency order.
> 2. **Course prerequisite sequencing** — academic audit systems schedule courses respecting pre-requisite chains.
> 3. **Task scheduling in project management** — PERT/CPM diagrams order project tasks respecting dependencies.
> 4. **Package dependency resolution** — tools like `apt` and `npm` install packages in topological order.
> 5. **Deadlock detection in OS** — absence of a valid topological order in a resource-allocation graph indicates a deadlock cycle.

---

### Q2. Write the **Master Theorem** statement and apply it to the recurrence $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$. State the resulting complexity. `(3 marks)`

**Model Answer:**

> **Master Theorem Statement:** For a recurrence of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$:
> - **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
> - **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.
> - **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ and the regularity condition holds, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
>
> **Application to $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$** (Strassen's):
> Here $a = 7$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.
> Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$.
> Since $f(n) = n^2 = O(n^{2.807 - 0.807}) = O(n^{\log_2 7 - 0.807})$, **Case 1** applies.
>
> $$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})}$$

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation (ESE) Pattern, Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q A.(a)** Explain **Topological Sorting** using **Kahn's Algorithm (Source Removal)** with a suitable example. State its time complexity. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Topological sorting produces a linear ordering $L$ of vertices of a DAG such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$, $u$ precedes $v$ in $L$. The ordering exists iff the graph has no directed cycles.

**Kahn's Algorithm — Step-by-Step Description (3 marks):**

> **Step 1 — In-degree computation:** For every vertex $v \in V$, count the number of edges entering $v$. This requires scanning all edges once: $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time.
>
> **Step 2 — Queue initialization:** Insert all vertices with in-degree 0 into a queue $Q$. This is the set of "source" vertices (no incoming dependencies).
>
> **Step 3 — Main processing loop:** While $Q$ is not empty:
> 1. Dequeue vertex $u$ from $Q$.
> 2. Append $u$ to the output ordering $L$.
> 3. For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$:
>    - Decrement $\text{indeg}(v)$ by 1.
>    - If $\text{indeg}(v)$ becomes 0, enqueue $v$.
>
> **Step 4 — Cycle detection:** After the loop, if $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$, then the graph contains a cycle and topological sort is impossible.

**Worked Example (2 marks):** Consider the DAG with edges $(1, 2)$, $(1, 3)$, $(2, 4)$, $(3, 4)$, $(3, 5)$, $(4, 6)$, $(5, 6)$.

Initial in-degrees: $1 \to 0$, $2 \to 1$, $3 \to 1$, $4 \to 2$, $5 \to 1$, $6 \to 2$.
Initial queue: $Q = \{1\}$.

| Step | Dequeue | Output $L$ | In-degree updates | New queue $Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | [1] | 2→0, 3→0 | {2, 3} |
| 2 | 2 | [1,2] | 4→1 | {3} |
| 3 | 3 | [1,2,3] | 4→0, 5→0 | {4, 5} |
| 4 | 4 | [1,2,3,4] | 6→1 | {5} |
| 5 | 5 | [1,2,3,4,5] | 6→0 | {6} |
| 6 | 6 | [1,2,3,4,5,6] | — | {} |

**Topological Order: $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.**

**Time Complexity (1 mark):**

$$T(n) = \Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

In-degree computation: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. Main loop: each vertex is enqueued and dequeued at most once, each edge is examined at most once. Hence total is $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.

---

#### **Q A.(b)** What is **Divide and Conquer (D&C)** strategy? Write the **Control Abstraction** for D&C. Apply it to derive the recurrence for **Merge Sort** and solve it using the **Master Theorem**. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**D&C Definition (1 mark):** Divide and Conquer is a recursive algorithm-design paradigm that:
1. **Divides** a problem of size $n$ into $a$ smaller sub-problems, each of size $n/b$.
2. **Conquers** by recursively solving each sub-problem.
3. **Combines** the sub-solutions to form the final answer.

**Control Abstraction (2 marks):** The control abstraction is a problem-independent algorithmic template:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: }\text{DandC}(P) \\
&1.\ \text{if } \text{small}(P)\ \text{then} \\
&2.\quad \text{return }\text{AdHoc}(P) \\
&3.\ \text{divide } P \text{ into } P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k \\
&4.\ \text{for } i = 1 \text{ to } k: \quad Y_i \leftarrow \text{DandC}(P_i) \\
&5.\ \text{return }\text{Combine}(Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_k)
\end{aligned}$$

Here $\text{small}(P)$ is true when the problem size falls below a threshold $n_0$ (base case), and $\text{Combine}$ is the algorithm-specific merge logic.

**Application to Merge Sort — Recurrence (2 marks):**
- **Divide:** Split array of size $n$ into 2 sub-arrays of size $n/2$ each.
- **Conquer:** Recursively sort each sub-array.
- **Combine:** Merge two sorted sub-arrays of total size $n$ in $\Theta(n)$ time.

Recurrence:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn, \quad T(1) = c$$

where $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$.

**Solving with Master Theorem (2 marks):**
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$.
- Compare $f(n) = cn$ with $n^{\log_b a} = n$.
- $f(n) = \Theta(n \log^0 n)$ matches Case 2 with $k = 0$.
- Therefore $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n \cdot \log^{0+1} 2) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

**Verification by recursion tree:**

| Level $i$ | # sub-problems | Size each | Cost per node | Total level cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | $n$ | $cn$ | $cn$ |
| 1 | 2 | $n/2$ | $c(n/2)$ | $cn$ |
| 2 | 4 | $n/4$ | $c(n/4)$ | $cn$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $\log_2 n$ | $n$ | 1 | $c$ | $cn$ |

Total: $T(n) = cn \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q B.(a)** Explain the **classical divide and conquer** approach for matrix multiplication. Show that it yields $\Theta(n^3)$ time. Then explain **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** algorithm, derive its recurrence, and solve it. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Classical Block MM (2 marks):** Let $A$, $B$ be $n \times n$ matrices. Partition into four $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ blocks $A_{ij}$, $B_{ij}$. The product $C = A \cdot B$ has four blocks:

$$C_{11} = A_{11}B_{11} + A_{12}B_{21}$$
$$C_{12} = A_{11}B_{12} + A_{12}B_{22}$$
$$C_{21} = A_{21}B_{11} + A_{22}B_{21}$$
$$C_{22} = A_{21}B_{12} + A_{22}B_{22}$$

This uses **8 sub-multiplications** of size $n/2$ matrices and **4 additions** of size $n/2$ matrices.

**Recurrence (1 mark):**

$$T(n) = 8 T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$$

Solving via Master Theorem Case 1: $n^{\log_2 8} = n^3 > n^2$, so $T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$.

**Strassen's Algorithm Idea (2 marks):** Strassen showed that 4 output blocks can be computed using **only 7 sub-multiplications** at the cost of extra additions/subtractions:

$$M_1 = (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22})$$
$$M_2 = (A_{21} + A_{22}) B_{11}$$
$$M_3 = A_{11} (B_{12} - B_{22})$$
$$M_4 = A_{22} (B_{21} - B_{11})$$
$$M_5 = (A_{11} + A_{12}) B_{22}$$
$$M_6 = (A_{21} - A_{11}) (B_{11} + B_{12})$$
$$M_7 = (A_{12} - A_{22}) (B_{21} + B_{22})$$

Output reconstruction (18 additions/subtractions total):

$$C_{11} = M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7$$
$$C_{12} = M_3 + M_5$$
$$C_{21} = M_2 + M_4$$
$$C_{22} = M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6$$

**Recurrence and Solution (2 marks):**

$$T(n) = 7 T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$$

Apply Master Theorem: $n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$, $f(n) = n^2 < n^{2.807}$, so **Case 1** applies:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})}$$

This beats the classical $\Theta(n^3)$ for sufficiently large $n$.

---

#### **Q B.(b)** Solve the following recurrences using the **Master Theorem**: `(7 marks)`

**(i)** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ `(2 marks)`
**(ii)** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ `(2 marks)`
**(iii)** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$ `(3 marks)`

**Model Solutions:**

**(i)** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$:

- $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.
- $f(n) = n = n^{1.0} = O(n^{2 - 1})$, so $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ with $\varepsilon = 1$.
- **Case 1 applies.**
- $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 4}) = \Theta(n^2)$.

**(ii)** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$:

- $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n \log n$.
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 2} = n$.
- $f(n) = n \log n = \Theta(n \log^1 n)$, matches Case 2 with $k = 1$.
- **Case 2 applies.**
- $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2} \cdot \log^{1+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)$.

**(iii)** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$:

- $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2 \log n$.
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.
- $f(n) = n^2 \log n = \Theta(n^2 \log^1 n)$, matches Case 2 with $k = 1$.
- **Case 2 applies.**
- $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 4} \cdot \log^{1+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^2 n)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Recurrence coefficients confusion**: Students often mis-identify $a$ and $b$. For $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$, remember $a$ is the **number of sub-problems** (7) and $b$ is the **size-divisor** (2), NOT the other way around. The critical exponent is $n^{\log_b a}$ — i.e., $\log_2 7$, not $\log_7 2$.
>
> 2. **Case 2 — missing the $+1$ in exponent**: The most common error in $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ is writing $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ but in the Master Theorem Case 2 with $k=0$, the result is $\Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^1 n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ — students sometimes write $\Theta(n \log^0 n) = \Theta(n)$, losing **3 full marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing $n^2$ vs $n^{\log_2 7}$ for Strassen's**: Strassen's wins asymptotically because $\log_2 7 \approx 2.807 > 2$. Students sometimes incorrectly conclude Strassen's is slower, arguing that $7$ multiplications are "obviously" more than $8$ — they forget that 7 multiplications happen on **half-sized** matrices, which compounds exponentially.
>
> 4. **Topological sort on cyclic graph**: If a student produces an ordering despite the graph having a cycle, the algorithm is **wrong by definition** — full marks deduction. Always check for cycles by counting if $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting auxiliary space analysis in Merge Sort**: Students often state "Merge Sort is $\Theta(n \log n)$" but forget to mention $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space — in KTU valuation, this loses at least 1 mark on questions asking for full complexity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (Module 2)**

### Topological Sort
- ☐ Defined **only for DAGs** (Directed Acyclic Graphs). Existence $\iff$ no directed cycle.
- ☐ Two algorithms: **Kahn's (Source Removal)** and **DFS-Based (Finish-Time Stack)** — both $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ Kahn's detects cycles naturally: $|\text{output}| < |V| \Rightarrow$ cycle exists.
- ☐ Topological order is **not unique** in general.
- ☐ Key applications: build systems, course prereqs, deadlock detection, package managers.

### Divide and Conquer Paradigm
- ☐ Three steps: **Divide → Conquer (recurse) → Combine**.
- ☐ **Control abstraction** is the generic D&C template — problem-independent skeleton.
- ☐ General time recurrence: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ for balanced D&C.
- ☐ D&C differs from DP: D&C sub-problems are **disjoint**; DP sub-problems **overlap**.
- ☐ Always identify the **base case** ($n_0$) and the **combine cost** $f(n)$ precisely.

### Merge Sort
- ☐ Stable, comparison-based, not in-place (requires $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space).
- ☐ Recurrence: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$, solving to $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- ☐ Merge step cost = $\Theta(n)$; tree depth = $\log_2 n$; **all levels cost $cn$** ⇒ $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- ☐ Best, worst, average case all $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- ☐ Preferred for **linked lists** and **external sorting** (tape/disk-based).

### Strassen's Matrix Multiplication
- ☐ Recurrence: $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$, solving to $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$.
- ☐ Uses **7 sub-multiplications** (vs classical 8) at the cost of **18 ±operations** (vs 4).
- ☐ The 7 products $M_1$ to $M_7$ must be memorized for the exam.
- ☐ Output reconstruction formulas: $C_{11} = M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7$, etc.
- ☐ Speedup: factor of $n^{0.193}$ over classical — significant only for very large $n$.
- ☐ **Numerically less stable** than classical (accumulates floating-point errors).

### Master Theorem
- ☐ Form: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$.
- ☐ **Case 1**: $f(n)$ polynomially smaller than $n^{\log_b a}$ ⇒ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- ☐ **Case 2**: $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ ⇒ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$. **Note the $+1$!**
- ☐ **Case 3**: $f(n)$ polynomially larger + regularity ⇒ $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- ☐ **Gap cases exist** — when $f(n)$ is not polynomially comparable, use recursion tree or Akra-Bazzi.
- ☐ Critical exponents to memorize: $\log_2 2 = 1$, $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585$, $\log_2 4 = 2$, $\log_2 7 \approx 2.807$, $\log_2 8 = 3$.

### Engineering Real-World Connections
- ☐ **Compiler design**: topological sort for instruction scheduling, Strassen's in scientific computing libraries (BLAS, LAPACK).
- ☐ **Graphics/Games**: matrix multiplication for transformations — Strassen's rarely used here due to small matrix sizes.
- ☐ **Database query optimization**: D&C merge joins.
- ☐ **Distributed systems**: D&C map-reduce paradigm.
- ☐ **Numerical HPC**: Strassen's + numerical stabilization (e.g., Bailey's variant) for supercomputing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greedy Strategy - Control Abstraction, Fractional Knapsack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Greedy Strategy - Control Abstraction, Fractional Knapsack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Greedy Strategy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Greedy Method** is an algorithmic paradigm that builds up a solution piece by piece, always choosing the next piece that offers the most obvious and immediate benefit (the *locally optimal choice*) at each step, with the unverified hope that this sequence of local optima will lead to a *globally optimal* solution.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education** framework, the Greedy Strategy is positioned as one of the three core algorithmic design paradigms (along with Divide & Conquer and Dynamic Programming). It is characterised by the following two essential mathematical properties:

- **Greedy Choice Property:** A globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice.
- **Optimal Substructure:** An optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to sub-problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cafeteria Tray" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you enter a buffet with a plate that can hold only **W** kilograms of food. You are presented with a counter of dishes, each having a certain *tastiness* (value) and *weight*. To get the **maximum tastiness** before your plate overflows, you would naturally scan the counter, pick the **tastiest-per-gram** dish first, fill your plate, then move to the next best ratio, and so on. You never re-evaluate a previously skipped dish. That sequential, "best-relative-bang-for-buck" decision-making process is exactly the **Greedy Strategy**.

In this analogy:
- **Tastiness per gram** = Value-to-Weight ratio (Density, $v_i / w_i$)
- **Plate capacity** = Knapsack capacity $W$
- **You can take partial servings** = The *Fractional* Knapsack variant

## 1.3 Control Abstraction — The Generic Skeleton

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Abstraction (KTU Terminology):** A *control abstraction* is a high-level, reusable algorithmic template that captures the structural skeleton common to all greedy algorithms. It hides the problem-specific `SELECT()` and `FEASIBLE()` sub-procedures behind a single uniform driver loop.

In essence, the **Control Abstraction for Greedy Algorithms** is a meta-algorithm:

1. Initialise the solution set as empty.
2. Repeatedly **SELECT** the most promising candidate from the remaining pool.
3. **VERIFY** that adding this candidate keeps the solution **FEASIBLE** (i.e., respects constraints).
4. If feasible, **INCLUDE** it; otherwise, **REJECT** it permanently.
5. Terminate when the candidate set is exhausted.

This abstraction allows KTU examiners to test whether students understand the *uniformity* behind seemingly different problems (Fractional Knapsack, Job Sequencing, Huffman Coding, Prim's/Kruskal's MST, Dijkstra's SSSP).

## 1.4 Fractional Knapsack — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fractional Knapsack Problem (FKNAP):**
> Given **n** items, each with weight $w_i > 0$ and value $v_i > 0$, and a knapsack of maximum capacity **W**, the objective is to select a quantity $x_i$ (where $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$) of each item $i$ such that the **total value** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i$ is **maximised**, subject to the **weight constraint** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W$.

The word **"Fractional"** is critical: unlike the 0/1 Knapsack, we are permitted to break items into arbitrary fractional pieces. This single relaxation is what makes the Greedy Strategy *provably optimal* for this variant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Greedy Choice

The greedy decision is never made arbitrarily. For **every** greedy algorithm, the student must be able to justify the selection criterion. The three classical selection criteria tested in KTU exams are:

- **Maximum Value First:** Pick the item with highest $v_i$ first.
- **Minimum Weight First:** Pick the item with lowest $w_i$ first.
- **Maximum Value Density First:** Pick the item with highest $v_i / w_i$ first.

For the **Fractional Knapsack**, only the third criterion (maximum density) is mathematically guaranteed to produce a globally optimal solution. The KTU examiner loves testing *why* the other two fail — a typical 7-mark sub-question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Maximum Density Works (The "Why" Behind the Greedy Choice):**
> Consider any two items $i$ and $j$ currently competing for the last unit of knapsack capacity. Suppose $v_i / w_i > v_j / w_j$. If an optimal solution *deprives* item $i$ in favour of item $j$, we can *swap* a unit of $j$ for a unit of $i$ and strictly increase the total value (or keep it equal). Since this swap argument can be applied inductively, the greedy density-based choice is always globally optimal. This is the **Exchange Argument**, a mandatory tool for any KTU proof question.

## 2.2 The Four Core Sub-Operations of the Control Abstraction

Every greedy algorithm can be decomposed into the following four functional blocks:

- **SELECT():** Choose the *best* remaining candidate according to the greedy metric. For FKNAP, this returns the item with the highest $v_i / w_i$ ratio.
- **FEASIBLE():** Test whether adding the selected candidate to the partial solution violates any constraint. For FKNAP, this checks $\sum w_i x_i + w_{\text{selected}} \leq W$.
- **ADD():** Incorporate the selected candidate into the solution set. For FKNAP, this may add a *fraction* $x_i \in (0, 1]$.
- **SOLUTION():** Determine when the algorithm should terminate. For FKNAP, this fires when the knapsack is full ($\sum w_i x_i = W$) or no candidates remain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "write the control abstraction for a greedy algorithm", students must use the *exact* identifier names — `Greedy(a, n)`, `SELECT`, `FEASIBLE`, `SOLUTION` — because these are the standard textbook conventions (Horowitz & Sahni / Cormen et al.) referenced by KTU 2024 Scheme textbooks.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, boundary condition, and complexity bound a KTU student must memorise for the Greedy Strategy module.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of items | $n$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Value of item $i$ | $v_i$ | Utility units (e.g., profit in Rs.) |
| Weight of item $i$ | $w_i$ | Mass units (e.g., kg) |
| Fraction selected of item $i$ | $x_i$, with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Knapsack capacity | $W$ | Same mass unit as $w_i$ |
| Value Density of item $i$ | $d_i = v_i / w_i$ | Value per unit weight |
| Objective Function | $\max \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i$ | Maximise total value |
| Weight Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W$ | Bound on total mass |
| Total Number of Variables | $n$ continuous decision variables | — |
| Sorting Cost (by density) | $O(n \log n)$ | Time complexity |
| Greedy Selection Cost | $O(n)$ | Time complexity |
| **Total FKNAP Complexity** | $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ | **Asymptotic upper bound** |
| Space Complexity | $O(1)$ auxiliary (in-place) | Extra memory |
| Dominance Condition | $d_a \geq d_b \Rightarrow$ select $a$ before $b$ | Sort ordering rule |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Fractional Knapsack has direct analogues in several engineering domains that KTU frequently highlights:

- **Cargo Loading in Logistics:** Optimising payload value within aircraft/truck weight limits where cargo can be split.
- **Resource Allocation in Cloud Computing:** Packing virtual machines (with fractional CPU shares) into physical servers.
- **Investment Portfolio Optimisation:** Allocating capital to projects with differing return-per-investment ratios.
- **Material Cutting in Manufacturing:** Selecting fractional lengths of raw stock to maximise sale value.

The **Greedy Control Abstraction** itself is the conceptual foundation for:
- **Huffman Coding** (data compression)
- **Dijkstra's Algorithm** (network routing)
- **Prim's & Kruskal's Algorithms** (minimum spanning trees in VLSI and telecom network design)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough of the Fractional Knapsack Algorithm

We will solve a classic FKNAP instance step-by-step. The exhaustive methodology below is the *gold standard* for full marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A thief breaks into a warehouse and finds the following items. The knapsack (bag) can carry a maximum weight of **W = 50 kg**. The thief wants to maximise the total value of items stolen, and crucially, **can take fractions of any item**.

| **Item $i$** | **Value $v_i$ (Rs.)** | **Weight $w_i$ (kg)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 60 | 10 |
| 2 | 100 | 20 |
| 3 | 120 | 30 |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Value Densities

We compute the value-to-weight ratio $d_i = v_i / w_i$ for every item. This is the *greedy metric*.

$$d_1 = \frac{v_1}{w_1} = \frac{60}{10} = 6.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{v_2}{w_2} = \frac{100}{20} = 5.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

$$d_3 = \frac{v_3}{w_3} = \frac{120}{30} = 4.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Sort Items in Decreasing Order of Density

After sorting, the priority queue (from highest density to lowest) is:

$$\text{Item 1 } (d=6.0) \; \succ \; \text{Item 2 } (d=5.0) \; \succ \; \text{Item 3 } (d=4.0)$$

For this particular instance, the items are *already* sorted, but in general the FKNAP algorithm must perform an explicit $O(n \log n)$ sort.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Greedy Selection Loop (Capacity = 50 kg)

**Iteration 1 — SELECT Item 1:**
- Remaining capacity: $W_{\text{rem}} = 50$ kg
- Weight of Item 1: $w_1 = 10$ kg
- Since $w_1 \leq W_{\text{rem}}$ ($10 \leq 50$), the item is *fully taken*.
- $x_1 = 1.0$, value gained = $v_1 \cdot x_1 = 60 \cdot 1.0 = 60$ Rs.
- $W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow 50 - 10 = 40$ kg.

**Iteration 2 — SELECT Item 2:**
- Remaining capacity: $W_{\text{rem}} = 40$ kg
- Weight of Item 2: $w_2 = 20$ kg
- Since $w_2 \leq W_{\text{rem}}$ ($20 \leq 40$), the item is *fully taken*.
- $x_2 = 1.0$, value gained = $v_2 \cdot x_2 = 100 \cdot 1.0 = 100$ Rs.
- $W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow 40 - 20 = 20$ kg.

**Iteration 3 — SELECT Item 3:**
- Remaining capacity: $W_{\text{rem}} = 20$ kg
- Weight of Item 3: $w_3 = 30$ kg
- Since $w_3 > W_{\text{rem}}$ ($30 > 20$), the item **cannot be fully taken**.
- We take a *fraction*: $x_3 = W_{\text{rem}} / w_3 = 20 / 30 = 2/3 \approx 0.6667$.
- Value gained = $v_3 \cdot x_3 = 120 \cdot (2/3) = 80$ Rs.
- $W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow 20 - 20 = 0$ kg.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Termination & Final Solution

The loop terminates because $W_{\text{rem}} = 0$, satisfying the SOLUTION() condition. The final selection vector is:

$$\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1.0,\ 1.0,\ 2/3)$$

The **maximum achievable value** is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\max} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} v_i x_i \\
&= v_1 x_1 + v_2 x_2 + v_3 x_3 \\
&= 60(1.0) + 100(1.0) + 120(2/3) \\
&= 60 + 100 + 80 \\
&= 240 \text{ Rs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **total weight** consumed is verified as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{used}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i x_i \\
&= 10(1.0) + 20(1.0) + 30(2/3) \\
&= 10 + 20 + 20 \\
&= 50 \text{ kg} = W \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimality Verification (Exchange Argument):** Had we instead taken all of Item 3 and a fraction of Item 2, the value would have been $120 + 100 \cdot (20/20) = 220$ Rs., which is strictly less than 240 Rs. Hence, the density-sorted greedy selection is indeed globally optimal.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Greedy vs. Other Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is a frequent source of 7-mark KTU questions asking students to *contrast* greedy with divide & conquer or dynamic programming.

| **Attribute** | **Greedy Method** | **Dynamic Programming** | **Divide & Conquer** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Reversibility | **Irreversible** — once chosen, never reconsidered | Reversible — all sub-problems solved | Reversible — sub-problems are disjoint |
| Optimal Substructure | Required | Required | Required |
| Greedy Choice Property | **Required** | Not required | Not required |
| Typical Time Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Memory Footprint | Low ($O(1)$ to $O(n)$) | High ($O(n^2)$ tables) | Moderate |
| Applicability to FKNAP | **Optimal** | Optimal (but wasteful) | Not natural |
| Risk of Sub-Optimality | High for some problems | None — guaranteed optimal | None — guaranteed optimal |

## 3.3 Algorithmic Pseudocode for Fractional Knapsack

The following is the **textbook-standard pseudocode** (no Python, no C) that KTU expects in the answer script:

```
ALGORITHM  : FractionalKnapsack(n, W, v[1..n], w[1..n])
INPUT       : n items, capacity W, value array v[], weight array w[]
OUTPUT      : Maximum value Zmax and selection vector x[1..n]

1.  for i ← 1 to n do
2.      d[i] ← v[i] / w[i]                    // Compute density
3.  end for
4.  Sort items in decreasing order of d[i]    // O(n log n)
5.  Wrem ← W                                  // Remaining capacity
6.  Zmax ← 0
7.  for i ← 1 to n do
8.      x[i] ← 0
9.      if w[i] ≤ Wrem then
10.         x[i] ← 1                          // Take whole item
11.         Wrem ← Wrem − w[i]
12.     else
13.         x[i] ← Wrem / w[i]                // Take fraction
14.         Wrem ← 0
15.     end if
16.     Zmax ← Zmax + v[i] * x[i]
17.     if Wrem = 0 then break
18. end for
19. RETURN (Zmax, x[1..n])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity Derivation (Line-by-Line):**
> - Lines 1–3: $O(n)$ — single linear pass to compute densities.
> - Line 4: $O(n \log n)$ — sorting dominates; the standard comparison sort lower bound applies.
> - Lines 5–18: $O(n)$ — single linear pass through sorted items.
> - **Total:** $T(n) = O(n) + O(n \log n) + O(n) = O(n \log n)$.
> - **Auxiliary Space:** $O(1)$ — only a constant number of scalar variables are introduced.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Generic Greedy Control Abstraction

The diagram below illustrates the uniform control flow that all greedy algorithms (including Fractional Knapsack) follow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Greedy a n] --> B[Initialise Solution S = empty]
    B --> C[Candidate set A is non empty]
    C -->|Yes| D[Call SELECT A returns best candidate x]
    D --> E[Call FEASIBLE S x returns true or false]
    E -->|True| F[Call ADD S x update solution set]
    E -->|False| G[Reject x permanently]
    F --> H[Call SOLUTION S returns true or false]
    G --> H
    H -->|True| I[Output final solution S]
    H -->|False| C
    C -->|No| I
    I --> J[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fractional Knapsack Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the interaction between the input layer, decision layer, and output layer of the FKNAP algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Item list n v1..vn w1..wn"]
        A2["Capacity W"]
    end
    subgraph DECISION["DECISION LAYER"]
        B1["Density Calculator d_i = v_i divided by w_i"]
        B2["Sorter decreasing order of d_i"]
        B3["Greedy Selector SELECT FEASIBLE ADD"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Selection vector x1..xn"]
        C2["Maximum value Zmax"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Iterative Greedy Loop for FKNAP

This sequence diagram shows the temporal ordering of operations for the worked example in Section 3.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver as GreedyDriver
    participant Selector as SELECT
    participant Checker as FEASIBLE
    participant Adder as ADD
    Driver->>Selector: Request best item
    Selector-->>Driver: Return Item1 d = 6.0
    Driver->>Checker: Test weight 10 against Wrem = 50
    Checker-->>Driver: Feasible true
    Driver->>Adder: Include Item1 x1 = 1.0
    Adder-->>Driver: Wrem = 40 Zmax = 60
    Driver->>Selector: Request best remaining item
    Selector-->>Driver: Return Item2 d = 5.0
    Driver->>Checker: Test weight 20 against Wrem = 40
    Checker-->>Driver: Feasible true
    Driver->>Adder: Include Item2 x2 = 1.0
    Adder-->>Driver: Wrem = 20 Zmax = 160
    Driver->>Selector: Request best remaining item
    Selector-->>Driver: Return Item3 d = 4.0
    Driver->>Checker: Test weight 30 against Wrem = 20
    Checker-->>Driver: Feasible false partial
    Driver->>Adder: Include fraction x3 = 2 divided by 3
    Adder-->>Driver: Wrem = 0 Zmax = 240
    Driver->>Driver: SOLUTION condition met terminate
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Why Density Sorting is Mandatory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Unsorted Items] --> Q[Step 1 Compute d_i for all i]
    Q --> R[Step 2 Sort by d_i descending]
    R --> S[Step 3 Greedy Loop top down]
    S --> T{Feasible Full Item}
    T -->|Yes| U[Take x_i = 1]
    T -->|No| V[Take fraction x_i = Wrem divided by w_i]
    U --> W{More Capacity}
    V --> W
    W -->|Yes| S
    W -->|No| X[Output Zmax and x vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the Exam Hall:** KTU expects students to draw **labelled rectangular blocks** connected by **directed arrows** for control abstraction questions. The use of `SELECT`, `FEASIBLE`, `ADD`, `SOLUTION` blocks is *non-negotiable* and is itself worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Greedy Strategy. State the two key properties a problem must satisfy for the greedy method to yield an optimal solution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Greedy Strategy** is an algorithmic design technique that constructs a solution iteratively by selecting the locally optimal choice at each step, without reconsidering previous decisions, in the hope of reaching a globally optimal solution.
>
> The two key properties are:
>
> 1. **Greedy Choice Property:** A globally optimal solution can be obtained by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice.
> 2. **Optimal Substructure:** An optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to its sub-problems.

*Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Property 1, 1 mark for Property 2.*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a control abstraction? Why is it useful in the context of greedy algorithms?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **control abstraction** is a high-level, reusable algorithmic template that captures the common structural skeleton of a class of algorithms while hiding the problem-specific sub-procedures behind named function calls.
>
> **Utility in Greedy Algorithms:** The control abstraction unifies the *apparently different* greedy algorithms (Fractional Knapsack, Job Sequencing, Huffman, MST) under a single conceptual framework with four reusable sub-operations: `SELECT`, `FEASIBLE`, `ADD`, and `SOLUTION`. This promotes modular design, code reuse, and easier verification of correctness.

*Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for utility explanation.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Solve the following Fractional Knapsack instance using the Greedy Method. Show all steps explicitly.**

| **Item** | **Value (Rs.)** | **Weight (kg)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 280 | 40 |
| B | 100 | 10 |
| C | 120 | 20 |
| D | 120 | 30 |

**Knapsack Capacity W = 50 kg.**

#### Part (a) — Compute densities, sort, and apply the greedy selection loop. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Value Densities $d_i = v_i / w_i$**

$$d_A = 280 / 40 = 7.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

$$d_B = 100 / 10 = 10.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

$$d_C = 120 / 20 = 6.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

$$d_D = 120 / 30 = 4.0 \text{ Rs./kg}$$

**Step 2: Sort in Decreasing Order of Density**

$$\text{B } (10.0) \;\succ\; \text{A } (7.0) \;\succ\; \text{C } (6.0) \;\succ\; \text{D } (4.0)$$

**Step 3: Greedy Selection Loop**

- **Iteration 1 (Item B):** $w_B = 10 \leq W_{\text{rem}} = 50$. Take $x_B = 1.0$. Value $= 100$. $W_{\text{rem}} = 40$.
- **Iteration 2 (Item A):** $w_A = 40 \leq W_{\text{rem}} = 40$. Take $x_A = 1.0$. Value $= 280$. $W_{\text{rem}} = 0$.

**Loop terminates** because $W_{\text{rem}} = 0$. Items C and D are not considered.

#### Part (b) — Report the final solution vector, maximum value, and verify weight constraint. (7 Marks)

**Final Selection Vector:**

$$\vec{x} = (x_A, x_B, x_C, x_D) = (1.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.0,\ 0.0)$$

**Maximum Total Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\max} &= \sum v_i x_i \\
&= 280(1.0) + 100(1.0) + 120(0.0) + 120(0.0) \\
&= 280 + 100 = \mathbf{380 \text{ Rs.}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Weight Constraint Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{used}} &= 40(1.0) + 10(1.0) + 20(0.0) + 30(0.0) \\
&= 50 \text{ kg} = W \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often forget to **explicitly state the sorted order** of items before starting the greedy loop. This omission costs **1 mark** immediately. Additionally, many students skip the **weight verification step** at the end, which is mandatory and worth **1 mark**.

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Write the general control abstraction for a greedy algorithm. Identify and explain the role of each of the four sub-procedures used.**

#### Part (a) — Write the generic control abstraction pseudocode and explain `SELECT` and `FEASIBLE`. (7 Marks)

**Generic Control Abstraction Pseudocode:**

```
ALGORITHM  : Greedy(a, n)
INPUT       : Set of candidates a of size n
OUTPUT      : Solution set S

1.  S ← empty
2.  while A ≠ empty and not SOLUTION(S) do
3.      x ← SELECT(A)               // Best candidate
4.      A ← A − {x}                 // Remove x from A
5.      if FEASIBLE(S, x) then
6.          S ← S ∪ {x}             // Add x to solution
7.      end if
8.  end while
9.  RETURN S
```

**Role of `SELECT(A)`:**
The `SELECT` procedure inspects the remaining candidate set $A$ and returns the *most promising* element $x$ according to the problem-specific greedy metric. For Fractional Knapsack, `SELECT` returns the unselected item with the highest value-to-weight ratio $d_i = v_i / w_i$. This is the *decision-making heart* of every greedy algorithm.

**Role of `FEASIBLE(S, x)`:**
The `FEASIBLE` procedure tests whether adding candidate $x$ to the partial solution $S$ would violate any of the problem's constraints. For Fractional Knapsack, `FEASIBLE` returns `true` if and only if the cumulative weight of items in $S$ plus $w_x$ does not exceed $W$. This acts as the *safety filter* that prevents constraint violations.

#### Part (b) — Explain `ADD` and `SOLUTION`, and prove that the Fractional Knapsack satisfies the Greedy Choice Property. (7 Marks)

**Role of `ADD(S, x)`:**
The `ADD` procedure formally incorporates the selected and approved candidate $x$ into the partial solution $S$, updating all relevant data structures. For Fractional Knapsack, `ADD` may insert either a *whole* item (if $w_x \leq W_{\text{rem}}$) or a *fractional* amount ($x_i = W_{\text{rem}} / w_x$). It also updates the running total $Z_{\max}$ and decrements $W_{\text{rem}}$.

**Role of `SOLUTION(S)`:**
The `SOLUTION` procedure is the *termination predicate*. It returns `true` when the partial solution $S$ is deemed complete and the algorithm should halt. For Fractional Knapsack, `SOLUTION` returns `true` when $W_{\text{rem}} = 0$ (knapsack full) or when $A = \emptyset$ (no candidates left).

**Proof of Greedy Choice Property for FKNAP:**

Consider any two items $i$ and $j$ with $d_i > d_j$. Suppose an optimal solution $S^*$ takes a fraction $x_j > 0$ of $j$ but takes less than the available amount of $i$ (i.e., $x_i < 1$ and $w_i x_i < w_i$). We can construct a new solution $S'$ by reducing $x_j$ by a small $\epsilon > 0$ and increasing $x_i$ by $\epsilon \cdot (w_j / w_i)$ to preserve the weight constraint. The change in total value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= (v_i \cdot \epsilon \cdot w_j / w_i) - (v_j \cdot \epsilon) \\
&= \epsilon \cdot (v_i w_j / w_i - v_j) \\
&= \epsilon \cdot w_j \cdot (v_i / w_i - v_j / w_j) \\
&= \epsilon \cdot w_j \cdot (d_i - d_j) > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $d_i > d_j$, we have $\Delta Z > 0$, meaning $S'$ is strictly better than $S^*$, contradicting the optimality of $S^*$. Therefore, an optimal solution must always prefer $i$ over $j$, validating the greedy choice. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> When asked to prove greedy optimality, students often *assert* the property without providing the **exchange argument** (a swap that strictly improves any non-greedy solution). Without this rigorous proof, **3 to 4 marks are forfeited** out of the 7 allocated to the proof sub-part. Always include the explicit $\Delta Z > 0$ derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:**
> Confusing the **0/1 Knapsack** (which is solved by *Dynamic Programming*, NOT Greedy) with the **Fractional Knapsack** (which IS solved by Greedy) is the single most common conceptual error. If a KTU question states *"items cannot be broken"*, the greedy method **fails** and the student must switch to DP. Misidentifying the variant costs **all 14 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Every Exam):**
>
> - **Greedy Strategy** makes **locally optimal, irreversible** choices aiming for a global optimum.
> - Two mandatory properties: **Greedy Choice Property** + **Optimal Substructure**.
> - The four pillars of the **Control Abstraction** are: `SELECT`, `FEASIBLE`, `ADD`, `SOLUTION`.
> - The **Fractional Knapsack** is solved by sorting items in **decreasing order of value density** $d_i = v_i / w_i$.
> - The objective is to **maximise** $\sum v_i x_i$ subject to $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$ and $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$.
> - The FKNAP greedy algorithm runs in **$O(n \log n)$** time, dominated by the sorting step.
> - The FKNAP greedy algorithm uses **$O(1)$** auxiliary space.
> - The greedy choice is optimal only because of the **Exchange Argument**: swapping a denser item in place of a less dense item strictly increases total value.
> - The **0/1 Knapsack is NOT a greedy problem** — it requires Dynamic Programming.
> - Always **verify the weight constraint** at the end of every FKNAP solution ($\sum w_i x_i = W$).
> - Always **show the sorted density order** explicitly — this is a 1-mark item examiners check first.
> - When asked for a **proof of optimality**, use the **exchange/swap argument** with an explicit $\Delta Z$ derivation.
> - The control abstraction diagram must contain the **four labelled blocks** — omissions lose 2–3 marks.
> - Real-world applications: cargo loading, cloud VM packing, portfolio optimisation, material cutting.
> - The Greedy Method is one of the **three pillars** of algorithm design (with D&C and DP); the comparison matrix in Section 3.2 is a frequently tested 7-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Cost Spanning Tree - Kruskal's and Prim's, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum Cost Spanning Tree - Kruskal's and Prim's, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spanning Tree** of a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ is a subgraph $T = (V, E')$ such that:
- $T$ includes **all** $n = \vert V \vert$ vertices of $G$
- $T$ is a **tree** (i.e., connected and acyclic)
- Therefore, $E'$ contains exactly $n-1$ edges

A **Minimum Cost Spanning Tree (MCST)**, often abbreviated as **MST (Minimum Spanning Tree)**, is a spanning tree whose sum of edge weights is **minimum** among all possible spanning trees of $G$:

$$w(T) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E'} w(u,v) \quad \text{is minimized}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The standard textbook terminology uses *Minimum Spanning Tree* (MST). The phrase *Minimum Cost Spanning Tree* is functionally identical. The terms are interchangeable in exams and valuation.

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are the **Chief Engineer of Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** tasked with supplying electricity to **5 towns** (Kochi, Thrissur, Palakkad, Kozhikode, Kannur) from a single power station. There are multiple possible road/cable routes between the towns, each with a different installation cost (₹ in crores).

- You **must** connect all 5 towns (cannot leave any without power).
- You **must not** create a loop/cycle (that would waste money on redundant cables).
- You want the **lowest possible total installation cost**.

This is exactly the MST problem! The towns are vertices, cable routes are weighted edges, and the MST is the cheapest network that still connects everything.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MST in Module 3 of DAA?**
> MST algorithms are flagship examples of the **Greedy Method** — a core algorithmic paradigm. Both Kruskal's and Prim's are greedy, but they make different local choices and use different data structures, making them ideal for studying algorithmic design trade-offs.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cut** | A partition of $V$ into two disjoint non-empty sets $S$ and $V \setminus S$ |
| **Crossing Edge** | An edge $(u,v)$ where $u \in S$ and $v \in V \setminus S$ |
| **Light Edge** | The minimum-weight crossing edge of a cut |
| **Cycle** | A closed path $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k = v_0$ with no repeated vertices except endpoints |
| **Safe Edge** | An edge that can be added to a partial MST without violating optimality |
| **Forest** | An acyclic graph (collection of disjoint trees) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Foundational Properties of MST

These properties are the **theoretical backbone** of both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms. KTU examiners frequently test them for 3-mark direct questions.

#### 2.1.1 The Cut Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of $G$, the **light edge** (minimum-weight crossing edge) belongs to **some** MST of $G$.

**Intuition:** When you "cut" the graph into two halves, the cheapest bridge connecting the two halves is always a safe (correct) choice — adding it to a growing MST will never make the final tree suboptimal.

#### 2.1.2 The Cycle Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the **maximum-weight edge** on $C$ does **NOT** belong to any MST of $G$ (provided edge weights are distinct; for equal weights, it need not appear in *every* MST, but is never forced to appear).

**Intuition:** Inside a cycle, the heaviest edge is always redundant. Remove it, and connectivity is preserved with lower cost.

### 2.2 Kruskal's Algorithm — Edge-Greedy Approach

**Strategy:** Build the MST by considering edges in **non-decreasing order of weight**. Add an edge if (and only if) it does not create a cycle with the edges already chosen.

**Required Data Structure:** **Disjoint Set Union (DSU)** with **Union by Rank** + **Path Compression** to detect cycles efficiently.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Sort all $m$ edges by weight in ascending order: $O(m \log m)$.
2. Initialize a DSU where each vertex is its own set (forest of singletons).
3. For each edge $(u, v, w)$ in sorted order:
   - If $\text{Find}(u) \neq \text{Find}(v)$ (i.e., $u$ and $v$ are in different components):
     - Add $(u, v)$ to the MST.
     - Perform $\text{Union}(u, v)$.
   - Else: discard the edge (it would form a cycle).
4. Stop when MST has $n-1$ edges.

### 2.3 Prim's Algorithm — Vertex-Greedy Approach

**Strategy:** Grow a single tree starting from an arbitrary root vertex. At each step, add the **minimum-weight edge** that connects a vertex already in the tree to a vertex outside it.

**Required Data Structure:** **Min-Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** or an **adjacency matrix** with key updates.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Pick an arbitrary root $r$. Initialize $\text{key}[v] = \infty$ for all $v$, $\text{key}[r] = 0$, and $\text{parent}[r] = \text{NIL}$.
2. Insert all vertices into a min-heap keyed by $\text{key}[v]$.
3. While the heap is not empty:
   - Extract the vertex $u$ with the **minimum key** from the heap.
   - Add the edge $(\text{parent}[u], u)$ to the MST (if $u \neq r$).
   - For each neighbor $v$ of $u$ still in the heap:
     - If $w(u, v) < \text{key}[v]$: update $\text{key}[v] = w(u, v)$ and $\text{parent}[v] = u$.
4. Stop when $n-1$ edges have been selected.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Kruskal's Algorithm | Prim's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Greedy (Edge-based) | Greedy (Vertex-based) |
| **Selection Criterion** | Globally minimum-weight edge not forming a cycle | Minimum-weight edge crossing the (tree, non-tree) cut |
| **Primary Data Structure** | Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find) | Min-Priority Queue (Binary/ Fibonacci Heap) |
| **Graph Type Preference** | **Sparse** graphs (efficient when $m \approx n$) | **Dense** graphs (efficient when $m \approx n^2$) |
| **Sorting Required** | Yes — sort all edges: $O(m \log m)$ | No sorting of edges |
| **Edge-List Input** | Naturally works | Needs adjacency conversion |
| **Time (Adjacency List + DSU)** | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(m \log n)$ (Binary Heap) |
| **Time (Adjacency List + Fibonacci Heap)** | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(m + n \log n)$ |
| **Time (Adjacency Matrix)** | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(n^2)$ — *no heap needed* |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n + m)$ for DSU + edge list | $O(n + m)$ for heap + adjacency |
| **Connectivity Check Needed** | Yes — if forest has multiple components, returns MSF | No — only works on connected graphs (else run from each component) |
| **Result for Disconnected Graph** | Returns a **Minimum Spanning Forest (MSF)** | Fails or requires multiple starts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Valuation Tip:** The most common mistake students make is writing the time complexity of Prim's as $O(n^2)$ without specifying the **implementation variant**. Always mention the **data structure used** alongside the complexity. For full marks in KTU 2024 ESE, the answer must explicitly tie complexity to the data structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

MST is not just a textbook concept. Its applications include:

- **Telecommunication Network Design:** Laying optical fiber to connect cities with minimum cable cost.
- **VLSI Circuit Design:** Minimizing wire length in chip layout.
- **Computer Network Routing:** Building low-cost broadcast trees.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Designing road/railway networks connecting cities.
- **Cluster Analysis:** Single-linkage clustering in data mining uses MST.
- **Image Segmentation & Handwriting Recognition:** MST-based graph cuts.
- **Approximation Algorithms:** MST-based 2-approximation for the NP-hard **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** in metric spaces.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Sample Weighted Graph

Consider the connected, undirected, weighted graph $G$ with $n = 6$ vertices and $m = 9$ edges.

**Vertex Set:** $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$

**Edge List (with weights):**

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (A, B) | 4 | (A, D) | 5 | (B, C) | 3 |
| (B, D) | 2 | (C, D) | 6 | (C, F) | 7 |
| (D, E) | 8 | (D, F) | 9 | (E, F) | 10 |

We need an MST with $n - 1 = 5$ edges.

### 3.2 Kruskal's Algorithm — Exhaustive Trace

**Step 1: Sort all edges by weight (ascending):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sorted Edge Order:} \quad & (B,D,2),\ (B,C,3),\ (A,B,4), \\
& (A,D,5),\ (C,D,6),\ (C,F,7), \\
& (D,E,8),\ (D,F,9),\ (E,F,10)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Initialize DSU.** Each vertex is its own parent: $\text{parent}[X] = X$.

**Step 3: Process each edge:**

**Iteration 1: Edge (B, D, 2)**
- $\text{Find}(B) = B$, $\text{Find}(D) = D$ → different sets.
- **Accept.** $\text{MST} = \{(B,D)\}$. Union(B, D) → representative = B.
- DSU after: $\{B, D\}$ as one component.

**Iteration 2: Edge (B, C, 3)**
- $\text{Find}(B) = B$, $\text{Find}(C) = C$ → different sets.
- **Accept.** $\text{MST} = \{(B,D),\ (B,C)\}$. Union(B, C) → representative = B.
- DSU after: $\{B, C, D\}$ as one component.

**Iteration 3: Edge (A, B, 4)**
- $\text{Find}(A) = A$, $\text{Find}(B) = B$ → different sets.
- **Accept.** $\text{MST} = \{(B,D),\ (B,C),\ (A,B)\}$. Union(A, B) → representative = B.
- DSU after: $\{A, B, C, D\}$ as one component.

**Iteration 4: Edge (A, D, 5)**
- $\text{Find}(A) = B$, $\text{Find}(D) = B$ → **same set**.
- **Reject** (forms cycle A-B-D-A).

**Iteration 5: Edge (C, D, 6)**
- $\text{Find}(C) = B$, $\text{Find}(D) = B$ → **same set**.
- **Reject** (forms cycle).

**Iteration 6: Edge (C, F, 7)**
- $\text{Find}(C) = B$, $\text{Find}(F) = F$ → different sets.
- **Accept.** $\text{MST} = \{(B,D),\ (B,C),\ (A,B),\ (C,F)\}$. Union(B, F).
- DSU after: $\{A, B, C, D, F\}$ as one component.

**Iteration 7: Edge (D, E, 8)**
- $\text{Find}(D) = B$, $\text{Find}(E) = E$ → different sets.
- **Accept.** $\text{MST} = \{(B,D),\ (B,C),\ (A,B),\ (C,F),\ (D,E)\}$.
- MST has $n - 1 = 5$ edges → **STOP**.

**Final MST (Kruskal's):**

$$
\text{MST} = \{(B,D, 2),\ (B,C, 3),\ (A,B, 4),\ (C,F, 7),\ (D,E, 8)\}
$$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(\text{MST}) &= 2 + 3 + 4 + 7 + 8 \\
&= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Prim's Algorithm — Exhaustive Trace

We use a **min-heap** keyed on $\text{key}[v]$. Initialize $\text{key}[A] = 0$ (root) and $\text{key}[X] = \infty$ for $X \neq A$.

**Initial Heap:** $\{(A, 0), (B, \infty), (C, \infty), (D, \infty), (E, \infty), (F, \infty)\}$.

**Iteration 1: Extract min → A (key = 0)**
- No parent (root). Add A to MST tree.
- Relax neighbors of A:
  - $B$: $\text{key}[B] = \min(\infty, w(A,B)) = 4$. $\text{parent}[B] = A$.
  - $D$: $\text{key}[D] = \min(\infty, w(A,D)) = 5$. $\text{parent}[D] = A$.
- Heap: $\{(B, 4), (D, 5), (C, \infty), (E, \infty), (F, \infty)\}$.

**Iteration 2: Extract min → B (key = 4)**
- Parent = A. Add edge $(A, B, 4)$ to MST.
- Relax neighbors of B:
  - $C$: $\text{key}[C] = \min(\infty, w(B,C)) = 3$. $\text{parent}[C] = B$.
  - $D$: $\text{key}[D] = \min(5, w(B,D)) = \min(5, 2) = 2$. $\text{parent}[D] = B$.
- Heap: $\{(D, 2), (C, 3), (E, \infty), (F, \infty)\}$.

**Iteration 3: Extract min → D (key = 2)**
- Parent = B. Add edge $(B, D, 2)$ to MST.
- Relax neighbors of D:
  - $C$: $\text{key}[C] = \min(3, 6) = 3$ (no change).
  - $E$: $\text{key}[E] = \min(\infty, 8) = 8$. $\text{parent}[E] = D$.
  - $F$: $\text{key}[F] = \min(\infty, 9) = 9$. $\text{parent}[F] = D$.
- Heap: $\{(C, 3), (E, 8), (F, 9)\}$.

**Iteration 4: Extract min → C (key = 3)**
- Parent = B. Add edge $(B, C, 3)$ to MST.
- Relax neighbors of C:
  - $F$: $\text{key}[F] = \min(9, 7) = 7$. $\text{parent}[F] = C$.
- Heap: $\{(F, 7), (E, 8)\}$.

**Iteration 5: Extract min → F (key = 7)**
- Parent = C. Add edge $(C, F, 7)$ to MST.
- Relax neighbors of F:
  - $E$: $\text{key}[E] = \min(8, 10) = 8$ (no change).
- Heap: $\{(E, 8)\}$.

**Iteration 6: Extract min → E (key = 8)**
- Parent = D. Add edge $(D, E, 8)$ to MST.
- MST now has 5 edges → **STOP**.

**Final MST (Prim's):**

$$
\text{MST} = \{(A, B, 4),\ (B, D, 2),\ (B, C, 3),\ (C, F, 7),\ (D, E, 8)\}
$$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(\text{MST}) &= 4 + 2 + 3 + 7 + 8 \\
&= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Both algorithms produced the **same total cost (24)**, which is expected because the MST is unique when all edge weights are distinct. If weights had ties, multiple valid MSTs could exist, and Kruskal's and Prim's might pick different (but equally optimal) ones.

### 3.4 Time Complexity Analysis Derivation

#### 3.4.1 Kruskal's Complexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Kruskal}}(n, m) &= \underbrace{O(m \log m)}_{\text{sort edges}} + \underbrace{O(m \cdot \alpha(n))}_{\text{$m$ DSU operations}} \\
&\approx O(m \log m) \quad \text{(since $\alpha(n) \le 4$ in practice)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\alpha(n)$ is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows so slowly it is treated as a constant.

#### 3.4.2 Prim's Complexity (Three Variants)

**Variant 1: Adjacency Matrix (no heap)**

$$
T_{\text{Prim}}^{(1)}(n, m) = O(n^2)
$$

Each extract-min scans all $n$ vertices; each update scans all $n$ vertices. Done $n$ times.

**Variant 2: Binary Min-Heap + Adjacency List**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Prim}}^{(2)}(n, m) &= \underbrace{O(n \log n)}_{\text{$n$ extract-min}} + \underbrace{O(m \log n)}_{\text{$m$ decrease-key}} \\
&= O((n + m) \log n) \\
&\approx O(m \log n) \quad \text{if graph is sparse}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variant 3: Fibonacci Heap + Adjacency List**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Prim}}^{(3)}(n, m) &= \underbrace{O(n \log n)}_{\text{$n$ extract-min (amortized)}} + \underbrace{O(m)}_{\text{$m$ decrease-key (amortized $O(1)$)}} \\
&= O(m + n \log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibonacci heaps** achieve the theoretically best bound $O(m + n \log n)$, but their high constant factors and implementation complexity often make binary heaps faster in practice on small/medium graphs.

### 4.2 Kruskal's Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> init1[Initialize DSU: each vertex its own parent]
    init1 --> sort1[Sort all edges in ascending order by weight]
    sort1 --> check1{All edges processed?}
    check1 -- No --> pick1[Pick next edge u,v,w]
    pick1 --> cycle1{Find u != Find v?}
    cycle1 -- Yes --> accept1[Add edge to MST]
    accept1 --> union1[Union u and v in DSU]
    union1 --> count1{Edge count = n minus 1?}
    count1 -- No --> check1
    count1 -- Yes --> done1([Return MST])
    cycle1 -- No --> reject1[Discard edge forms cycle]
    reject1 --> check1
    check1 -- Yes --> done1
```

### 4.3 Prim's Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([Start]) --> root2[Pick arbitrary root r and set key r = 0]
    root2 --> heap2[Insert all vertices into min-heap keyed by key]
    heap2 --> check2{Heap empty?}
    check2 -- No --> extract2[Extract vertex u with minimum key]
    extract2 --> add2[Add edge parent u, u to MST]
    add2 --> relax2[For each neighbor v of u still in heap]
    relax2 --> cond2{w u,v less than key v?}
    cond2 -- Yes --> update2[Update key v = w u,v and parent v = u]
    update2 --> check2
    cond2 -- No --> check2
    check2 -- Yes --> done2([Return MST])
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: MST Solver Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        ipt1[Graph G = V,E,w]
    end

    subgraph Engine
        eng1[Algorithm Selector: Kruskal or Prim]
        eng2[Edge Sorter Module]
        eng3[DSU Union-Find Core]
        eng4[Min-Priority Queue Core]
    end

    subgraph Output
        out1[MST Edge Set]
        out2[Total Weight]
    end

    ipt1 --> eng1
    eng1 -->|Kruskal| eng2
    eng2 --> eng3
    eng1 -->|Prim| eng4
    eng3 --> out1
    eng3 --> out2
    eng4 --> out1
    eng4 --> out2
```

```mermaid
        P2[Uses Min-Heap]
    end

    R --> O[Output: MST]
```
