**Random Variable Setup (1 Mark):** Let $X$ be the number of attempts until the first success. Then:

$$
X \sim \text{Geometric}(p)
$$

**Step 1 — Mean of Geometric RV (2 Marks):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p
$$

Standard result (proved via differentiation of geometric series):

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] \;=\; \frac{1}{p}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Expected Time (1 Mark):** By linearity of expectation, since each attempt takes $O(g(n))$ deterministic time:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\,T(n)\,] \;=\; \mathbb{E}[X] \cdot O(g(n)) \;=\; \frac{O(g(n))}{p}
$$

**Step 3 — Asymptotic Conclusion (1 Mark):** If $p = \Omega(1)$ (a constant independent of $n$), then:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}[\,T(n)\,] \;=\; O(g(n)) \cdot O(1) \;=\; O(g(n))\;}
$$

Hence the algorithm runs in expected polynomial time when the per-attempt success probability is non-vanishing.

**Application:** Randomized Quicksort has $p \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$ (random pivot is good with constant probability) and $g(n) = O(n \log n)$, giving $\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Block Diagram & Examples**

**Monte Carlo Flow (3 Marks):**
1. Input $x$.
2. Draw random $r$.
3. Deterministic computation produces candidate $y$.
4. Output $y$ — **no verification**.
5. Running time is bounded and deterministic; correctness holds w.p. $\geq 1 - \epsilon$.

**Las Vegas Flow (3 Marks):**
1. Input $x$.
2. Loop:
   a. Draw random $r$.
   b. Compute candidate $y$.
   c. Verify $y$ using a checker.
   d. If verified, exit loop with $y$.
3. Output $y$ — **always correct**; expected iterations $\mathbb{E}[X] = 1/p$.

**Examples (1 Mark):**

| **Class** | **Algorithm** | **Why it fits** |
|---|---|---|
| Monte Carlo | **Freivalds' matrix multiplication verifier** | Verifies $A \cdot B = C$ in $O(n^2)$ with one-sided error $\leq \tfrac{1}{2}$ |
| Las Vegas | **Randomized Quicksort** | Always outputs sorted array; expected $O(n \log n)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (MUST READ):**
> 1. **Confusing MC and LV (–2 Marks):** Students often mix up the two classes. Memory trick: **MC = Money (result) is uncertain, Clock (time) is fixed**; **LV = Luck (time) varies, but Victory (result) is certain**.
> 2. **Forgetting independence in error amplification (–1 Mark):** When deriving $\epsilon_{\text{new}} = \epsilon^{k}$, the KTU examiner expects an explicit mention of **independent** repetitions. Without independence, the product rule does not hold.
> 3. **Forgetting the per-attempt time in LV analysis (–1 Mark):** Always state $T(n) = X \cdot O(g(n))$ and apply linearity of expectation, *not* just $\mathbb{E}[X]$ alone.
> 4. **Skipping the verifier mention for LV (–1 Mark):** Every Las Vegas algorithm must have an **efficient verifier**; otherwise, the "re-randomize and retry" strategy is meaningless.
> 5. **Not distinguishing one-sided vs two-sided MC error (–1 Mark):** This is a sub-classification; KTU 2024 frequently tests it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes (Save for Last-Minute Prep):**
>
> - **Randomized Algorithm** = deterministic algorithm + random-number generator primitive.
> - **Monte Carlo (MC):** *Time* deterministic (worst-case poly), *Result* correct w.p. $\geq 1 - \epsilon$.
> - **Las Vegas (LV):** *Result* always correct (= 1), *Time* random with bounded expectation.
> - **Memory Trick:** **MC = Maybe Correct**; **LV = Lucky Variable-time**.
> - **Error Amplification (MC, one-sided):** $\epsilon_{\text{new}} = \epsilon^{k}$ via independent repetitions and **OR** aggregation.
> - **Error Amplification (MC, two-sided):** Use **majority vote** + **Chernoff bound** $\Rightarrow \epsilon_{\text{new}} \leq \exp(-2k\delta^{2})$.
> - **Expected Time of LV (Geometric retries):** $\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = \dfrac{O(g(n))}{p}$.
> - **LV needs an efficient verifier** for the re-randomization loop to be meaningful.
> - **MC examples:** Miller–Rabin (one-sided), Freivalds' (one-sided), Schwartz–Zippel PIT (two-sided), Karger's min-cut.
> - **LV examples:** Randomized Quicksort, Randomized QuickSelect, randomized cache replacement.
> - **Complexity Classes:** $\mathbf{BPP}$ = MC-type bounded-error; $\mathbf{ZPP}$ = LV-type zero-error. $\mathbf{ZPP} = \mathbf{RP} \cap \mathbf{coRP}$.
> - **Conversion Theorem:** MC with efficient verifier $\Rightarrow$ LV by amplification; under $\mathbf{BPP} \subseteq \mathbf{ZPP}$ (open in general, but holds for many natural problems), LV $\Leftrightarrow$ MC.
> - **KTU-Favoured Distinction Line:** "Monte Carlo *approximates correctness in fixed time*; Las Vegas *guarantees correctness in expected time*."
> - **One-sided error MC examples almost always relate to primality / matrix verification.** Remember them.
> - **For 14-mark questions**, always include: (i) formal definition, (ii) success probability / expected time, (iii) one numerical worked example (e.g., $\epsilon = \tfrac{1}{4}$, $k = 20$), (iv) comparison table, (v) flow diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized Version of Quicksort Algorithm with Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT743 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Version of Quicksort Algorithm with Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Randomized Quicksort** is a probabilistic variant of the standard deterministic Quicksort algorithm in which the **pivot element** for each recursive partition step is selected *uniformly at random* from the subarray being sorted, rather than following a fixed deterministic rule (such as choosing the first, last, or middle element). The randomization is applied *internally* by the algorithm itself, making its expected running time independent of any specific input ordering supplied by an adversary.

Formally, given an input array $A[p \dots r]$, Randomized Quicksort invokes the procedure `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)`, which first calls `RANDOM(A, r)` to select a pivot index $i \in [p, r]$ with **uniform probability** $1/(r-p+1)$, swaps $A[i]$ with $A[r]$, and then executes the deterministic `PARTITION(A, p, r)` routine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **class teacher** who wants to fairly pick one student each day to be the class leader. If you always pick the student sitting in the **first bench, first row** (a deterministic rule), then a mischievous student could keep sitting there and be chosen every day — a *worst-case scenario*. 

To avoid this, you **close your eyes and point randomly** at any student. No matter how the students arrange themselves, every student has an equal chance of being chosen. Even if the same mischievous student keeps sitting at the front, the next day's choice is independent of his position. 

**Randomized Quicksort behaves identically:** instead of always picking the first or last element as pivot (which an adversarial input can force to be the worst case), the algorithm *randomizes* the pivot selection so that *no input distribution* can systematically defeat it. The expected performance becomes a property of the algorithm's own randomness — not of the input given to it.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Deterministic Algorithm**: Output is fully determined by the input. The same input always produces the same execution path.
> - **Randomized Algorithm**: Uses random bits during execution, so the same input can produce different execution paths and the running time becomes a *random variable*.
> - **Expected Running Time**: Average running time taken over the algorithm's *own internal random choices*, not over the distribution of inputs.
> - **Las Vegas Algorithm** *(this is one!)*: A randomized algorithm that **always** produces a correct result, but whose *running time* is a random variable. Randomized Quicksort is the canonical Las Vegas algorithm.
> - **Monte Carlo Algorithm**: Produces a result whose *correctness* (not time) is random.
> - **Uniform Random Selection**: Every element in the subarray is chosen as pivot with **equal probability $1/n$** for an array of $n$ elements.

## 1.4 Why Randomization is Needed in Quicksort

Standard Quicksort has the following behaviour depending on pivot choice:

| Pivot Strategy | Best Case | Worst Case | Triggered by |
|---|---|---|---|
| First / Last element | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Already-sorted / reverse-sorted input |
| Median-of-three | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Carefully engineered inputs |
| **Random pivot** | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ *with negligible probability* | Probability $1/n!$ — astronomically small |

The crucial insight: the **expected** running time of Randomized Quicksort is $\Theta(n \lg n)$ for *any* input — sorted, reverse-sorted, or adversarial — because the expectation is taken over the algorithm's internal coin flips, not over the input distribution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

The Randomized Quicksort procedure operates in three tightly-coupled subroutines:

### Step 1: `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)`
- **Base case**: If $p < r$, partition and recurse. If $p \geq r$, the subarray has $\leq 1$ element and is already sorted — return.
- **Recursive step**: Call `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)` which returns the final pivot position $q$.
- Recurse on the left subarray `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q-1)`.
- Recurse on the right subarray `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q+1, r)`.

### Step 2: `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)`
- Compute a random pivot index: $i \leftarrow \text{RANDOM}(r-p+1) + p$.
- **Swap** $A[i]$ with $A[r]$ so the randomly-chosen element is now at the end (preparing for the standard linear-scan partition).
- Return the result of the deterministic `PARTITION(A, p, r)`.

### Step 3: `PARTITION(A, p, r)` *(standard Hoare/Lomuto scheme)*
- Choose $x \leftarrow A[r]$ as the pivot value.
- Maintain index $i \leftarrow p - 1$ for the boundary of the "less-than" region.
- For $j$ from $p$ to $r-1$: if $A[j] \leq x$, increment $i$ and swap $A[i]$ with $A[j]$.
- Finally swap $A[i+1]$ with $A[r]$ and return $i+1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the swap with $A[r]$ matters:** The deterministic `PARTITION` routine always assumes the pivot is the *last* element. By placing the randomly-chosen element at position $r$ *before* calling `PARTITION`, we preserve the integrity of the linear partition procedure while injecting randomness into the pivot choice.

## 2.2 The `RANDOM` Subroutine

To sample uniformly from $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1\}$:

$$
\text{RANDOM}(n) = (r + 1) \bmod n
$$

where $r$ is a state variable initialized to a random seed and updated after each call. This is the **linear congruential generator** approach — produces a uniform-looking stream of integers in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. Master the table; the derivations follow.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pivot selection probability | $\Pr[\text{element } A[i] \text{ is chosen}] = \dfrac{1}{r-p+1}$ | Uniform over subarray of size $n = r-p+1$ |
| 2 | Indicator random variable | $X_{ij} = \mathbb{I}\{z_i \text{ is compared to } z_j\}$ | $z_1 < z_2 < \dots < z_n$ in sorted order |
| 3 | Total comparisons | $X = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}$ | Sum over all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs |
| 4 | Expected comparisons | $T(n) = \mathbb{E}[X] = \displaystyle\sum_{i<j} \Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j]$ | Linearity of expectation |
| 5 | Pairwise comparison probability | $\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = \dfrac{2}{j-i+1}$ | Derived via the "first pivot wins" argument |
| 6 | Recurrence for $T(n)$ | $T(n) = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{q=0}^{n-1} \bigl[T(q) + T(n-q-1)\bigr] + (n-1)$ | $(n-1)$ is the partition cost |
| 7 | Asymptotic bound | $T(n) \leq 2n \ln n \approx 1.39 \, n \lg n$ | Upper bound using harmonic series |
| 8 | Lower bound | $T(n) \geq c \cdot n \lg n$ | For some constant $c > 0$ |
| 9 | Worst-case probability | $\Pr[T(n) > c \cdot n \lg n] < \dfrac{1}{n^2}$ | Vanishingly small via Markov's inequality |
| 10 | Worst-case running time | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Same as deterministic, but with tiny probability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Randomized Quicksort is the **workhorse sorting routine in the standard libraries of virtually every major programming language** — it is the default in C's `qsort`, Java's `Arrays.sort` (for primitive types), and Python's old `sort` (TimSort is the modern alternative). Its $\Theta(n \lg n)$ expected time, $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$ stack depth, and **cache-friendly in-place partitioning** make it indispensable in:
- **Database engines** for in-memory index sorting.
- **Operating systems** for process scheduling lists.
- **Numerical libraries** for eigenvalue routines requiring internal sort calls.
- **Network packet classifiers** for high-throughput rule matching.

The randomization guarantees that even an attacker crafting pathological inputs cannot degrade a server's performance — a property called **adversarial robustness** that deterministic sorting cannot offer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup: Expected Running Time via Indicator Random Variables

Let the input array elements, when sorted, be denoted $z_1 < z_2 < \dots < z_n$. Define an **indicator random variable** for every unordered pair $\{i, j\}$ with $i < j$:

$$
X_{ij} = \mathbb{I}\{z_i \text{ is ever compared to } z_j \text{ during Randomized Quicksort}\}
$$

That is, $X_{ij} = 1$ if at any recursive level the two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ are placed in the same subarray and one of them is chosen as pivot, and $X_{ij} = 0$ otherwise. The total number of comparisons performed by the algorithm is the random variable:

$$
X = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}
$$

By the **linearity of expectation** (which does *not* require independence of the $X_{ij}$):

$$
T(n) = \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_{ij}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j]
$$

So the problem reduces to finding $\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j]$ for any pair with $i < j$.

## 3.2 Lemma: The "First Pivot Wins" Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lemma.** For $i < j$, the elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared during the execution of Randomized Quicksort **if and only if** one of the following elements is the *first* pivot chosen from the set $Z_{ij} = \{z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j\}$:
> - $z_i$ itself, **or**
> - $z_j$ itself.

**Intuition (the "Why"):**

At each recursive call, a pivot is chosen uniformly at random from the current subarray. After the partition step, the pivot is placed in its final sorted position and is **never touched again**. The two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ therefore *remain together* in the same subarray **until and only until** one of them is chosen as a pivot. The moment *any* element $z_k$ with $i < k < j$ is chosen as pivot, $z_i$ goes to the left subarray and $z_j$ goes to the right subarray — they are forever separated and will never be compared.

Hence $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared $\iff$ the *first* pivot drawn from $Z_{ij}$ is either $z_i$ or $z_j$.

**Computing the probability:**

The set $Z_{ij}$ contains exactly $k = j - i + 1$ elements. Each element is equally likely to be the first pivot chosen from this set. Therefore:

$$
\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = \Pr[\text{first pivot from } Z_{ij} \text{ is } z_i \text{ or } z_j] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

This is the key result — it depends *only* on the rank difference $j - i + 1$, not on the absolute values of $i$ and $j$.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Expected Running Time

Starting from the expression derived above:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

**Substitution step:** Let $k = j - i + 1$. Then $j = i + k - 1$, and as $j$ ranges from $i+1$ to $n$, the new index $k$ ranges from $2$ to $n - i + 1$.

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{k=2}^{n-i+1} \frac{2}{k}
$$

**Switching the order of summation:** For a fixed value of $k$, the variable $i$ must satisfy $i \leq n - k + 1$, i.e., $i$ ranges from $1$ to $n - k + 1$. So:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=2}^{n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n-k+1} 1 \right) \cdot \frac{2}{k} = \sum_{k=2}^{n} (n - k + 1) \cdot \frac{2}{k}
$$

**Algebraic expansion:** Write $(n - k + 1) = (n + 1) - k$ and split:

$$
T(n) = 2 \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{n + 1 - k}{k} = 2 \sum_{k=2}^{n} \left( \frac{n+1}{k} - 1 \right)
$$

**Separating the sums:**

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1) \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} \; - \; 2 \sum_{k=2}^{n} 1
$$

The second sum is just $2(n-1)$. For the first sum, we recognise the **harmonic number** $H_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 1/k$:

$$
\sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} = H_n - 1
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1)(H_n - 1) - 2(n-1)
$$

**Simplification:**

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1) H_n - 2(n+1) - 2n + 2
$$

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1) H_n - 4n
$$

**Bounding with the integral approximation:** For the $n$-th harmonic number:

$$
H_n = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \dots + \frac{1}{n} = \ln n + \gamma + \frac{1}{2n} - \frac{1}{12n^2} + \dots
$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the **Euler–Mascheroni constant**. Using the standard bound $\ln(n+1) \leq H_n \leq \ln n + 1$:

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1) H_n - 4n \leq 2(n+1)(\ln n + 1) - 4n
$$

$$
T(n) \leq 2n \ln n + 2 \ln n + 2n + 2 - 4n
$$

$$
T(n) \leq 2n \ln n - 2n + 2 \ln n + 2
$$

For $n \geq 2$, the lower-order terms are dominated, giving the celebrated result:

$$
\boxed{\,T(n) \leq 2n \ln n \approx 1.386 \, n \lg n\,}
$$

Converting to base-2 logarithms: $\ln n = (\lg n)(\ln 2)^{-1} \approx 1.4427 \lg n$, so $2n \ln n \approx 2.885 n \lg n$ — though the more conservative $1.39 n \lg n$ bound is obtained by tighter bounding.

## 3.4 The Partition-Cost Recurrence (Alternative Derivation)

The total cost of Randomized Quicksort can be expressed directly as a recurrence by conditioning on which element is chosen as the first pivot:

$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{q=0}^{n-1} \bigl[ T(q) + T(n - q - 1) \bigr] + \Theta(n)
$$

Here, $q$ is the number of elements less than the pivot (which is uniform over $\{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$), the two recursive calls sort the left and right partitions of sizes $q$ and $n - q - 1$, and the $\Theta(n) = (n-1)$ term accounts for the linear-time partitioning work. By the symmetry of the sum $\sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(n-q-1) = \sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(q)$:

$$
T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(q) + (n - 1)
$$

Solving this recurrence (via induction, induction hypothesis $T(q) \leq 2q \ln q$ for $q < n$) confirms the same $\Theta(n \lg n)$ asymptotic bound.

## 3.5 Probabilistic Tail Bound on Worst-Case Behaviour

Although the *expected* time is $\Theta(n \lg n)$, the algorithm can occasionally take longer. Using **Markov's inequality** on the over-scaled running time $4n \lg n$:

$$
\Pr[T(n) > 2 \cdot 2n \lg n] < \frac{\mathbb{E}[T(n)]}{4n \lg n} \leq \frac{2n \ln n}{4n \lg n} = \frac{\ln 2}{2} < 1
$$

The probability that the actual time exceeds twice the expectation is strictly less than $1/2$ — and by a more refined analysis (using the union bound over all recursive levels), the probability that the total time ever exceeds $c \cdot n \lg n$ for a suitable constant $c$ is less than $1/n^2$. This makes the worst-case $\Theta(n^2)$ behaviour **practically impossible**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Deterministic vs. Randomized Quicksort

| Metric | Deterministic Quicksort | Randomized Quicksort |
|---|---|---|
| Pivot choice | Fixed rule (first/last/middle) | Uniformly random |
| Best case | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ |
| Expected case | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ for *random* input | $\Theta(n \lg n)$ for *any* input |
| Worst case | $\Theta(n^2)$ on sorted/reverse-sorted | $\Theta(n^2)$ with probability $\leq 1/n!$ |
| Adversarial resistance | Poor — easy to attack | Excellent — attack requires predicting coin flips |
| Space complexity | $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$ expected, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ worst |
| Type | Deterministic | **Las Vegas** (always correct, time is random) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Recursion Flow of Randomized Quicksort

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Array A p to r] --> B{Check p less than r}
    B -- No --> C[Return: Subarray size zero or one, already sorted]
    B -- Yes --> D[Call RANDOMIZED PARTITION A p r]
    D --> E[Generate random pivot index i in range p to r]
    E --> F[Swap A i with A r]
    F --> G[Run deterministic PARTITION A p r]
    G --> H[Receive pivot final position q]
    H --> I[Recursive call on left: RANDOMIZED QUICKSORT A p q minus 1]
    H --> J[Recursive call on right: RANDOMIZED QUICKSORT A q plus 1 r]
    I --> K[Continue recursion until base case]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Sorted output returned]
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Partition Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Input subarray A p to r] --> S2[Pick random index i uniformly]
    S2 --> S3[Swap A i and A r]
    S3 --> S4[Pivot x is now A r]
    S4 --> S5[Initialize i pointer at p minus 1]
    S5 --> S6[Scan j from p to r minus 1]
    S6 --> S7{Check A j less than or equal to x}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Increment i and swap A i with A j]
    S7 -- No --> S6
    S8 --> S6
    S6 --> S9[End of scan: place pivot at A i plus 1]
    S9 --> S10[Return final pivot index i plus 1]
```

## 4.3 Recursive Partitioning Tree — Conceptual View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Level 0: Full array of n elements, random pivot chosen] --> R1L[Left subarray after partition]
    R0 --> R1R[Right subarray after partition]
    R1L --> R2LL[Left of left subarray]
    R1L --> R2LR[Right of left subarray]
    R1R --> R2RL[Left of right subarray]
    R1R --> R2RR[Right of right subarray]
    R2LL --> R3L1[Recursion depth increases]
    R2LL --> R3L2[Recursion depth increases]
    R2LR --> R3L3[Recursion depth increases]
    R2LR --> R3L4[Recursion depth increases]
    R2RL --> R3R1[Recursion depth increases]
    R2RL --> R3R2[Recursion depth increases]
    R2RR --> R3R3[Recursion depth increases]
    R2RR --> R3R4[Recursion depth increases]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the tree:** Each node represents one call to `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION`. The *expected* depth of this tree is $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$, which is why the auxiliary stack space is also $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$ on average. The *worst-case* depth is $n$ (giving $\mathcal{O}(n)$ stack space), but this happens with vanishingly small probability.

## 4.4 Comparison Probability Diagram — The Pairwise Argument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Elements z i to z j in current subarray] --> P1{First pivot chosen from this set}
    P1 -- Pivot is z i --> C1[Comparison happens between z i and z j]
    P1 -- Pivot is z j --> C1
    P1 -- Pivot is some z k with i less than k less than j --> N1[No comparison: elements separated into different partitions]
    C1 --> Out1[Probability equals 2 divided by j minus i plus 1]
    N1 --> Out2[Probability equals j minus i minus 1 divided by j minus i plus 1]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is a *randomized algorithm*? How does Randomized Quicksort differ from the standard deterministic Quicksort? Justify why the randomized version is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

A randomized algorithm is one whose behaviour is determined not only by its input but also by values produced by a *random-number generator* during execution. The running time becomes a *random variable* whose expectation is taken over the algorithm's internal coin flips.

In **deterministic Quicksort**, the pivot is always chosen by a fixed rule (e.g., first or last element). An adversary can supply a specific input ordering (already-sorted, reverse-sorted, or specially crafted) that forces the algorithm into its $\Theta(n^2)$ worst case.

In **Randomized Quicksort**, the pivot is selected uniformly at random from the subarray using a call to `RANDOM`. This decouples the pivot choice from the *structure of the input*, so the same adversarial input cannot systematically cause poor partitions. As a result, the *expected* running time is $\Theta(n \lg n)$ for *every* possible input — making it the preferred choice in production systems like C's `qsort`.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** State and prove the lemma used to compute the expected number of comparisons in Randomized Quicksort for any two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ (with $i < j$).

**Model Answer:**

**Lemma:** Two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ ($i < j$) are compared during the execution of Randomized Quicksort **if and only if** one of them is the *first* pivot chosen from the set $\{z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j\}$.

**Proof (Sketch):** After a pivot $x$ is chosen and the partition is performed, $x$ is placed in its final sorted position and never recursed into. The two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ remain in the *same* subarray only as long as no pivot from the set $\{z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j\}$ has been chosen. If the *first* such pivot is $z_i$ or $z_j$, they are compared against each other (since the other is in the same subarray). If the first pivot is some $z_k$ with $i < k < j$, then $z_i$ and $z_j$ are split into different subarrays and can never meet again. The set has $j - i + 1$ elements, so the probability that the first pivot is $z_i$ or $z_j$ is:

$$
\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION` and `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT` procedures with neat pseudocode. State the role of the `RANDOM` subroutine. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**`RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)`**

```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
1.  if p < r
2.      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
3.      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
4.      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)
```

**`RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)`**

```
RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
1.  i = RANDOM(r - p + 1) + p        // uniform index in [p, r]
2.  exchange A[i] with A[r]            // place pivot at end
3.  return PARTITION(A, p, r)
```

**`PARTITION(A, p, r)` (Lomuto scheme)**

```
PARTITION(A, p, r)
1.  x = A[r]                           // pivot value
2.  i = p - 1
3.  for j = p to r - 1
4.      if A[j] <= x
5.          i = i + 1
6.          exchange A[i] with A[j]
7.  exchange A[i + 1] with A[r]
8.  return i + 1
```

**Role of the `RANDOM` subroutine:**

The `RANDOM(n)` function returns a uniform integer in $\{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$. Within `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION`, the call `RANDOM(r - p + 1) + p` produces a uniform pivot index in $\{p, p+1, \dots, r\}$. The randomness ensures that pivot selection is *input-independent*, so no input ordering can be adversarial to the algorithm. The call takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ expected time using a pseudo-random number generator such as a linear congruential generator: $\text{RANDOM}(n) = (r+1) \bmod n$, where the global state $r$ is updated after every call.

#### (b) Perform a complete expected-time analysis of Randomized Quicksort using indicator random variables. Show that $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \lg n)$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Let $z_1 < z_2 < \dots < z_n$ be the elements in sorted order. Define the indicator random variable:

$$
X_{ij} = \mathbb{I}\{z_i \text{ is compared to } z_j\}, \quad 1 \le i < j \le n
$$

The total number of comparisons is:

$$
X = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}
$$

By linearity of expectation:

$$
T(n) = \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j]
$$

By the **first-pivot-wins lemma**, two elements are compared iff one of them is the first pivot from the set $Z_{ij} = \{z_i, \dots, z_j\}$, which has size $j - i + 1$:

$$
\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

Let $k = j - i + 1$ (so $k$ ranges from $2$ to $n - i + 1$):

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{k=2}^{n-i+1} \frac{2}{k} = \sum_{k=2}^{n} (n - k + 1) \cdot \frac{2}{k}
$$

Splitting $(n-k+1) = (n+1) - k$:

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1) \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} - 2 \sum_{k=2}^{n} 1
$$

Recognising the harmonic number $H_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 1/k$:

$$
T(n) = 2(n+1)(H_n - 1) - 2(n-1) = 2(n+1) H_n - 4n
$$

Using the bound $H_n \leq \ln n + 1$:

$$
T(n) \leq 2(n+1)(\ln n + 1) - 4n \leq 2n \ln n + 2 \ln n + 2n + 2 - 4n
$$

For $n \geq 2$, the lower-order terms are dominated, giving:

$$
\boxed{\,T(n) \leq 2n \ln n = \mathcal{O}(n \lg n)\,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### (a) With a neat algorithm, explain the procedure of Randomized Quicksort. Also explain the difference between a Las Vegas algorithm and a Monte Carlo algorithm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
1.  if p < r
2.      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
3.      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
4.      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)
```

```
RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
1.  i = RANDOM(r - p + 1) + p
2.  exchange A[i] with A[r]
3.  return PARTITION(A, p, r)
```

**Working Principle:** The algorithm picks a *random pivot*, partitions the array into two subarrays (one with elements $\leq$ pivot, the other with elements $>$ pivot), and recursively sorts the subarrays. The pivot element is in its final sorted position after each partition call.

**Las Vegas vs. Monte Carlo Algorithms:**

| Property | Las Vegas | Monte Carlo |
|---|---|---|
| Output | **Always correct** | Correct with some probability |
| Running time | **Random variable** | Deterministic (bounded) |
| Example | **Randomized Quicksort** | Primality testing (Miller–Rabin) |
| Guarantee | Time may vary; result is reliable | Time is fixed; result may be wrong |
| Re-execution strategy | Re-run if too slow | Re-run only for verification |

Randomized Quicksort is a **Las Vegas algorithm** because it always produces a correctly sorted array — only its *execution time* is a random variable.

#### (b) For an input of $n = 8$ elements, compute the expected number of comparisons in Randomized Quicksort. Show all steps. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Using the formula:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}
$$

For $n = 8$:

**Pair $(i, j)$ table** showing contribution $\frac{2}{j-i+1}$:

| $i$ | $j$ | $j - i + 1$ | Contribution $2/(j-i+1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 3 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 1 | 4 | 4 | 0.5000 |
| 1 | 5 | 5 | 0.4000 |
| 1 | 6 | 6 | 0.3333 |
| 1 | 7 | 7 | 0.2857 |
| 1 | 8 | 8 | 0.2500 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 2 | 5 | 4 | 0.5000 |
| 2 | 6 | 5 | 0.4000 |
| 2 | 7 | 6 | 0.3333 |
| 2 | 8 | 7 | 0.2857 |
| 3 | 4 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 3 | 5 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 3 | 6 | 4 | 0.5000 |
| 3 | 7 | 5 | 0.4000 |
| 3 | 8 | 6 | 0.3333 |
| 4 | 5 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 4 | 6 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 4 | 7 | 4 | 0.5000 |
| 4 | 8 | 5 | 0.4000 |
| 5 | 6 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 5 | 7 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 5 | 8 | 4 | 0.5000 |
| 6 | 7 | 2 | 1.0000 |
| 6 | 8 | 3 | 0.6667 |
| 7 | 8 | 2 | 1.0000 |

**Row-by-row sum for each $i$:**

- $i = 1$: $1 + 2/3 + 1/2 + 2/5 + 1/3 + 2/7 + 1/4 = 3.4298$
- $i = 2$: $1 + 2/3 + 1/2 + 2/5 + 1/3 + 2/7 = 3.1798$
- $i = 3$: $1 + 2/3 + 1/2 + 2/5 + 1/3 = 2.9000$
- $i = 4$: $1 + 2/3 + 1/2 + 2/5 = 2.5667$
- $i = 5$: $1 + 2/3 + 1/2 = 2.1667$
- $i = 6$: $1 + 2/3 = 1.6667$
- $i = 7$: $1 = 1.0000$

**Total expected comparisons:**

$$
T(8) = 3.4298 + 3.1798 + 2.9000 + 2.5667 + 2.1667 + 1.6667 + 1.0000 \approx 16.91
$$

**Verification via the closed form** $T(n) = 2(n+1)H_n - 4n$:

$$
H_8 = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + 1/5 + 1/6 + 1/7 + 1/8 = 2.7179
$$

$$
T(8) = 2 \cdot 9 \cdot 2.7179 - 4 \cdot 8 = 48.9222 - 32 = 16.92
$$

Both methods agree: $T(8) \approx 16.92$ comparisons.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Randomized Quicksort Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the swap in `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION`:** Many students write `i = RANDOM(r-p+1) + p` but forget to swap $A[i]$ with $A[r]$ before calling `PARTITION`. The deterministic `PARTITION` expects the pivot at the **last position** $A[r]$; without the swap, the algorithm selects a *non-pivot* element for partitioning. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing "expected over input" with "expected over coin flips":** The expected running time is an average over the algorithm's *own randomness*, not over a distribution on the inputs. Phrases like "average case of Quicksort" are **wrong** in the context of randomized algorithms. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Skipping the linearity-of-expectation step:** The indicator-variable method works *without* independence only because linearity of expectation doesn't need independence. Students often incorrectly invoke independence; this loses method credit. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Wrong harmonic-number bound:** Some students use $H_n \approx \lg n$ (base-2) instead of $\ln n$ (base-$e$). The final answer must use $2n \ln n$ or its base-2 equivalent $\approx 1.39 \, n \lg n$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Missing the "first pivot wins" justification:** Stating the formula $\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = 2/(j-i+1)$ without the pairwise-argument proof is incomplete for full marks. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 6. **Confusing pivot positions with pivot *values*:** The index returned by `PARTITION` is the *final position* of the pivot, not the pivot's value. Recursion boundaries are $p$ to $q-1$ and $q+1$ to $r$. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Randomized Quicksort:**

- **Definition:** Randomized Quicksort selects the pivot *uniformly at random* from the current subarray, then partitions and recurses. Class: **Las Vegas Algorithm**.

- **Three core procedures:** `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT`, `RANDOMIZED-PARTITION`, and `PARTITION`. Always remember the **swap of $A[i]$ with $A[r]$** before partitioning.

- **Indicator random variable:** $X_{ij} = \mathbb{I}\{z_i \text{ compared to } z_j\}$. The expectation uses **linearity of expectation** — no independence assumption needed.

- **Key Lemma ("First Pivot Wins"):** $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared $\iff$ the first pivot drawn from $\{z_i, \dots, z_j\}$ is $z_i$ or $z_j$. Hence $\Pr[z_i \text{ compared to } z_j] = \dfrac{2}{j-i+1}$.

- **Closed-form result:** $T(n) = 2(n+1)H_n - 4n$, where $H_n$ is the $n$-th harmonic number.

- **Asymptotic bound:** $T(n) \leq 2n \ln n = \mathcal{O}(n \lg n)$. The $\ln$ is base-$e$ (natural log); for base-2, $T(n) \approx 1.39 \, n \lg n$.

- **Recurrence form:** $T(n) = \dfrac{2}{n} \sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(q) + (n-1)$, with the $(n-1)$ accounting for partition cost.

- **Worst-case tail bound:** $\Pr[T(n) > c \cdot n \lg n] < 1/n^2$ via Markov's inequality and the union bound over $\mathcal{O}(n)$ recursive levels.

- **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\lg n)$ expected stack depth, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ worst-case stack depth.

- **Adversarial robustness:** Unlike deterministic Quicksort, the worst-case $\Theta(n^2)$ behaviour is *not triggered* by any specific input — it is triggered by an *astronomically unlikely* sequence of internal coin flips.

- **Practical use:** Powers `qsort` in C, primitive-type sorting in Java, and several high-performance numerical libraries. The randomization guarantees predictable performance in production systems facing untrusted or adversarial inputs.

- **Compare and contrast:** Randomized Quicksort vs. Deterministic Quicksort — same expected time, but randomized version is **input-distribution-independent**; Monte Carlo vs. Las Vegas — Randomized Quicksort is **Las Vegas** (always correct, time is random).
